units.speaker: ANDROMACHE
units.text:
When was my lord so much ungently temper'd,
To stop his ears against admonishment?
Unarm, unarm, and do not fight to-day.

units.speaker: HECTOR
units.text:
You train me to offend you; get you in:
By all the everlasting gods, I'll go!

units.speaker: ANDROMACHE
units.text: My dreams will, sure, prove ominous to the day.

units.speaker: HECTOR
units.text: No more, I say.
Enter CASSANDRA

units.speaker: CASSANDRA
units.text: Where is my brother Hector?

units.speaker: ANDROMACHE
units.text:
Here, sister; arm'd, and bloody in intent.
Consort with me in loud and dear petition,
Pursue we him on knees; for I have dream'd
Of bloody turbulence, and this whole night
Hath nothing been but shapes and forms of slaughter.

units.speaker: CASSANDRA
units.text: O, 'tis true.

units.speaker: HECTOR
units.text: Ho! bid my trumpet sound!

units.speaker: CASSANDRA
units.text: No notes of sally, for the heavens, sweet brother.

units.speaker: HECTOR
units.text: Be gone, I say: the gods have heard me swear.

units.speaker: CASSANDRA
units.text:
The gods are deaf to hot and peevish vows:
They are polluted offerings, more abhorr'd
Than spotted livers in the sacrifice.

units.speaker: ANDROMACHE
units.text:
O, be persuaded! do not count it holy
To hurt by being just: it is as lawful,
For we would give much, to use violent thefts,
And rob in the behalf of charity.

units.speaker: CASSANDRA
HECTOR
units.text:
Hold you still, I say;
Mine honour keeps the weather of my fate:
Life every man holds dear; but the brave man
Holds honour far more precious-dear than life.
Enter TROILUS

units.speaker: HECTOR
units.text: How now, young man! mean'st thou to fight to-day?

units.speaker: ANDROMACHE
units.text: Cassandra, call my father to persuade.
Exit CASSANDRA

units.speaker: HECTOR
units.text:
No, faith, young Troilus; doff thy harness, youth;
I am to-day i' the vein of chivalry:
Let grow thy sinews till their knots be strong,
And tempt not yet the brushes of the war.
Unarm thee, go, and doubt thou not, brave boy,
I'll stand to-day for thee and me and Troy.

units.speaker: TROILUS
units.text:
Brother, you have a vice of mercy in you,
Which better fits a lion than a man.

units.speaker: HECTOR
units.text: What vice is that, good Troilus? chide me for it.

units.speaker: TROILUS
units.text:
When many times the captive Grecian falls,
Even in the fan and wind of your fair sword,
You bid them rise, and live.

units.speaker: HECTOR
units.text: O,'tis fair play.

units.speaker: TROILUS
units.text: Fool's play, by heaven, Hector.

units.speaker: HECTOR
units.text: How now! how now!

units.speaker: TROILUS
units.text:
For the love of all the gods,
Let's leave the hermit pity with our mothers,
And when we have our armours buckled on,
The venom'd vengeance ride upon our swords,
Spur them to ruthful work, rein them from ruth.

units.speaker: HECTOR
units.text: Fie, savage, fie!

units.speaker: TROILUS
units.text: Hector, then 'tis wars.

units.speaker: HECTOR
units.text: Troilus, I would not have you fight to-day.

units.speaker: TROILUS
units.text:
Who should withhold me?
Not fate, obedience, nor the hand of Mars
Beckoning with fiery truncheon my retire;
Not Priamus and Hecuba on knees,
Their eyes o'ergalled with recourse of tears;
Not you, my brother, with your true sword drawn,
Opposed to hinder me, should stop my way,
But by my ruin.
Re-enter CASSANDRA, with PRIAM

units.speaker: CASSANDRA
units.text:
Lay hold upon him, Priam, hold him fast:
He is thy crutch; now if thou lose thy stay,
Thou on him leaning, and all Troy on thee,
Fall all together.

units.speaker: PRIAM
units.text:
Come, Hector, come, go back:
Thy wife hath dream'd; thy mother hath had visions;
Cassandra doth foresee; and I myself
Am like a prophet suddenly enrapt
To tell thee that this day is ominous:
Therefore, come back.

units.speaker: HECTOR
units.text:
AEneas is a-field;
And I do stand engaged to many Greeks,
Even in the faith of valour, to appear
This morning to them.

units.speaker: PRIAM
units.text: Ay, but thou shalt not go.

units.speaker: HECTOR
units.text:
I must not break my faith.
You know me dutiful; therefore, dear sir,
Let me not shame respect; but give me leave
To take that course by your consent and voice,
Which you do here forbid me, royal Priam.

units.speaker: CASSANDRA
units.text: O Priam, yield not to him!

units.speaker: ANDROMACHE
units.text: Do not, dear father.

units.speaker: HECTOR
units.text:
Andromache, I am offended with you:
Upon the love you bear me, get you in.
Exit ANDROMACHE

units.speaker: TROILUS
units.text:
This foolish, dreaming, superstitious girl
Makes all these bodements.

units.speaker: CASSANDRA
units.text:
O, farewell, dear Hector!
Look, how thou diest! look, how thy eye turns pale!
Look, how thy wounds do bleed at many vents!
Hark, how Troy roars! how Hecuba cries out!
How poor Andromache shrills her dolours forth!
Behold, distraction, frenzy and amazement,
Like witless antics, one another meet,
And all cry, Hector! Hector's dead! O Hector!

units.speaker: TROILUS
units.text: Away! away!

units.speaker: CASSANDRA
units.text:
Farewell: yet, soft! Hector! take my leave:
Thou dost thyself and all our Troy deceive.
Exit

units.speaker: HECTOR
units.text:
You are amazed, my liege, at her exclaim:
Go in and cheer the town: we'll forth and fight,
Do deeds worth praise and tell you them at night.

units.speaker: PRIAM
units.text: Farewell: the gods with safety stand about thee!
Exeunt severally PRIAM and HECTOR. Alarums

units.speaker: TROILUS
units.text:
They are at it, hark! Proud Diomed, believe,
I come to lose my arm, or win my sleeve.
Enter PANDARUS

units.speaker: PANDARUS
units.text: Do you hear, my lord? do you hear?

units.speaker: TROILUS
units.text: What now?

units.speaker: PANDARUS
units.text: Here's a letter come from yond poor girl.

units.speaker: TROILUS
units.text: Let me read.

units.speaker: PANDARUS
units.text:
A whoreson tisick, a whoreson rascally tisick so
troubles me, and the foolish fortune of this girl;
and what one thing, what another, that I shall
leave you one o' these days: and I have a rheum
in mine eyes too, and such an ache in my bones
that, unless a man were cursed, I cannot tell what
to think on't. What says she there?

units.speaker: TROILUS
units.text:
Words, words, mere words, no matter from the heart:
The effect doth operate another way.

units.speaker: TROILUS

units.text: Exeunt severally
Alarums: excursions. Enter THERSITES

units.speaker: THERSITES
units.text:
Now they are clapper-clawing one another; I'll go
look on. That dissembling abominable varlet Diomed,
has got that same scurvy doting foolish young knave's
sleeve of Troy there in his helm: I would fain see
them meet; that that same young Trojan ass, that
loves the whore there, might send that Greekish
whore-masterly villain, with the sleeve, back to the
dissembling luxurious drab, of a sleeveless errand.
O' the t'other side, the policy of those crafty
swearing rascals, that stale old mouse-eaten dry
cheese, Nestor, and that same dog-fox, Ulysses, is
not proved worthy a blackberry: they set me up, in
policy, that mongrel cur, Ajax, against that dog of
as bad a kind, Achilles: and now is the cur Ajax
prouder than the cur Achilles, and will not arm
to-day; whereupon the Grecians begin to proclaim
barbarism, and policy grows into an ill opinion.
Soft! here comes sleeve, and t'other.
Enter DIOMEDES, TROILUS following

units.speaker: TROILUS
units.text:
Fly not; for shouldst thou take the river Styx,
I would swim after.

units.speaker: DIOMEDES
units.text:
Thou dost miscall retire:
I do not fly, but advantageous care
Withdrew me from the odds of multitude:
Have at thee!

units.speaker: THERSITES
units.text:
Hold thy whore, Grecian!—now for thy whore,
Trojan!—now the sleeve, now the sleeve!
Exeunt TROILUS and DIOMEDES, fighting
Enter HECTOR

units.speaker: HECTOR
units.text:
What art thou, Greek? art thou for Hector's match?
Art thou of blood and honour?

units.speaker: THERSITES
units.text:
No, no, I am a rascal; a scurvy railing knave:
a very filthy rogue.

units.speaker: HECTOR
units.text: I do believe thee: live.
Exit

units.speaker: THERSITES
units.text:
God-a-mercy, that thou wilt believe me; but a
plague break thy neck for frightening me! What's
become of the wenching rogues? I think they have
swallowed one another: I would laugh at that
miracle: yet, in a sort, lechery eats itself.
I'll seek them.
Exit
Enter DIOMEDES and a Servant

units.speaker: DIOMEDES
SERVANT
units.text: I go, my lord.
Exit
Enter AGAMEMNON

units.speaker: AGAMEMNON
units.text:
Renew, renew! The fierce Polydamas
Hath beat down Menon: bastard Margarelon
Hath Doreus prisoner,
And stands colossus-wise, waving his beam,
Upon the pashed corpses of the kings
Epistrophus and Cedius: Polyxenes is slain,
Amphimachus and Thoas deadly hurt,
Patroclus ta'en or slain, and Palamedes
Sore hurt and bruised: the dreadful Sagittary
Appals our numbers: haste we, Diomed,
To reinforcement, or we perish all.
Enter NESTOR

units.speaker: NESTOR
units.text:
Go, bear Patroclus' body to Achilles;
And bid the snail-paced Ajax arm for shame.
There is a thousand Hectors in the field:
Now here he fights on Galathe his horse,
And there lacks work; anon he's there afoot,
And there they fly or die, like scaled sculls
Before the belching whale; then is he yonder,
And there the strawy Greeks, ripe for his edge,
Fall down before him, like the mower's swath:
Here, there, and every where, he leaves and takes,
Dexterity so obeying appetite
That what he will he does, and does so much
That proof is call'd impossibility.
Enter ULYSSES

units.speaker: ULYSSES
units.text:
O, courage, courage, princes! great Achilles
Is arming, weeping, cursing, vowing vengeance:
Patroclus' wounds have roused his drowsy blood,
Together with his mangled Myrmidons,
That noseless, handless, hack'd and chipp'd, come to
him,
Crying on Hector. Ajax hath lost a friend
And foams at mouth, and he is arm'd and at it,
Roaring for Troilus, who hath done to-day
Mad and fantastic execution,
Engaging and redeeming of himself
With such a careless force and forceless care
As if that luck, in very spite of cunning,
Bade him win all.
Enter AJAX

units.speaker: AJAX
units.text: Troilus! thou coward Troilus!
Exit

units.speaker: DIOMEDES
units.text: Ay, there, there.

units.speaker: NESTOR
units.text: So, so, we draw together.
Enter ACHILLES

units.speaker: ACHILLES
units.text:
Where is this Hector?
Come, come, thou boy-queller, show thy face;
Know what it is to meet Achilles angry:
Hector? where's Hector? I will none but Hector.
Exeunt
Enter AJAX

units.speaker: AJAX
units.text: Troilus, thou coward Troilus, show thy head!
Enter DIOMEDES

units.speaker: DIOMEDES
units.text: Troilus, I say! where's Troilus?

units.speaker: AJAX
units.text: What wouldst thou?

units.speaker: DIOMEDES
units.text: I would correct him.

units.speaker: AJAX
units.text:
Were I the general, thou shouldst have my office
Ere that correction. Troilus, I say! what, Troilus!
Enter TROILUS

units.speaker: TROILUS
units.text:
O traitor Diomed! turn thy false face, thou traitor,
And pay thy life thou owest me for my horse!

units.speaker: DIOMEDES
units.text: Ha, art thou there?

units.speaker: AJAX
units.text: I'll fight with him alone: stand, Diomed.

units.speaker: DIOMEDES
units.text: He is my prize; I will not look upon.

units.speaker: TROILUS
units.text: Come, both you cogging Greeks; have at you both!
Exeunt, fighting
Enter HECTOR

units.speaker: HECTOR
units.text: Yea, Troilus? O, well fought, my youngest brother!
Enter ACHILLES

units.speaker: ACHILLES
units.text: Now do I see thee, ha! have at thee, Hector!

units.speaker: HECTOR
units.text: Pause, if thou wilt.

units.speaker: ACHILLES
units.text:
I do disdain thy courtesy, proud Trojan:
Be happy that my arms are out of use:
My rest and negligence befriends thee now,
But thou anon shalt hear of me again;
Till when, go seek thy fortune.
Exit

units.speaker: HECTOR
units.text:
Fare thee well:
I would have been much more a fresher man,
Had I expected thee. How now, my brother!
Re-enter TROILUS

units.speaker: TROILUS
units.text:
Ajax hath ta'en AEneas: shall it be?
No, by the flame of yonder glorious heaven,
He shall not carry him: I'll be ta'en too,
Or bring him off: fate, hear me what I say!
I reck not though I end my life to-day.
Exit
Enter one in sumptuous armour

units.speaker: HECTOR
units.text:
Stand, stand, thou Greek; thou art a goodly mark:
No? wilt thou not? I like thy armour well;
I'll frush it and unlock the rivets all,
But I'll be master of it: wilt thou not,
beast, abide?
Why, then fly on, I'll hunt thee for thy hide.
Exeunt
Enter ACHILLES, with Myrmidons

units.speaker: ACHILLES
units.text:
Come here about me, you my Myrmidons;
Mark what I say. Attend me where I wheel:
Strike not a stroke, but keep yourselves in breath:
And when I have the bloody Hector found,
Empale him with your weapons round about;
In fellest manner execute your aims.
Follow me, sirs, and my proceedings eye:
It is decreed Hector the great must die.
Exeunt
Enter MENELAUS and PARIS, fighting: then THERSITES

units.speaker: THERSITES
units.text:
The cuckold and the cuckold-maker are at it. Now,
bull! now, dog! 'Loo, Paris, 'loo! now my double-
henned sparrow! 'loo, Paris, 'loo! The bull has the
game: ware horns, ho!
Exeunt PARIS and MENELAUS
Enter MARGARELON

units.speaker: MARGARELON
units.text: Turn, slave, and fight.

units.speaker: THERSITES
units.text: What art thou?

units.speaker: MARGARELON
units.text: A bastard son of Priam's.

units.speaker: THERSITES
units.text:
I am a bastard too; I love bastards: I am a bastard
begot, bastard instructed, bastard in mind, bastard
in valour, in every thing illegitimate. One bear will
not bite another, and wherefore should one bastard?
Take heed, the quarrel's most ominous to us: if the
son of a whore fight for a whore, he tempts judgment:
farewell, bastard.
Exit

units.speaker: MARGARELON
units.text: The devil take thee, coward!
Exit
Enter HECTOR

units.speaker: HECTOR
units.text:
Most putrefied core, so fair without,
Thy goodly armour thus hath cost thy life.
Now is my day's work done; I'll take good breath:
Rest, sword; thou hast thy fill of blood and death.
Enter ACHILLES and Myrmidons

units.speaker: ACHILLES
units.text:
Look, Hector, how the sun begins to set;
How ugly night comes breathing at his heels:
Even with the vail and darking of the sun,
To close the day up, Hector's life is done.

units.speaker: HECTOR
units.text: I am unarm'd; forego this vantage, Greek.

units.speaker: ACHILLES
units.text: Strike, fellows, strike; this is the man I seek.
HECTOR falls

units.speaker: ACHILLES
units.text:
So, Ilion, fall thou next! now, Troy, sink down!
Here lies thy heart, thy sinews, and thy bone.
On, Myrmidons, and cry you all amain,
'Achilles hath the mighty Hector slain.'
A retreat sounded

units.speaker: ACHILLES
units.text: Hark! a retire upon our Grecian part.

units.speaker: MYRMIDONS
units.text: The Trojan trumpets sound the like, my lord.

units.speaker: ACHILLES
units.text:
The dragon wing of night o'erspreads the earth,
And, stickler-like, the armies separates.
My half-supp'd sword, that frankly would have fed,
Pleased with this dainty bait, thus goes to bed.

units.speaker: ACHILLES
units.text:
Come, tie his body to my horse's tail;
Along the field I will the Trojan trail.
Exeunt
Enter AGAMEMNON, AJAX, MENELAUS, NESTOR, DIOMEDES, and others, marching. Shouts within

units.speaker: AGAMEMNON
units.text: Hark! hark! what shout is that?

units.speaker: NESTOR
units.text: Peace, drums!
Within

units.speaker: NESTOR
units.text: Achilles! Achilles! Hector's slain! Achilles.

units.speaker: DIOMEDES
units.text: The bruit is, Hector's slain, and by Achilles.

units.speaker: AJAX
units.text:
If it be so, yet bragless let it be;
Great Hector was a man as good as he.

units.speaker: AGAMEMNON
units.text:
March patiently along: let one be sent
To pray Achilles see us at our tent.
If in his death the gods have us befriended,
Great Troy is ours, and our sharp wars are ended.
Exeunt, marching
Enter AENEAS and Trojans

units.speaker: AENEAS
units.text:
Stand, ho! yet are we masters of the field:
Never go home; here starve we out the night.
Enter TROILUS

units.speaker: TROILUS
units.text: Hector is slain.

units.speaker: ALL
units.text: Hector! the gods forbid!

units.speaker: TROILUS
units.text:
He's dead; and at the murderer's horse's tail,
In beastly sort, dragg'd through the shameful field.
Frown on, you heavens, effect your rage with speed!
Sit, gods, upon your thrones, and smile at Troy!
I say, at once let your brief plagues be mercy,
And linger not our sure destructions on!

units.speaker: AENEAS
units.text: My lord, you do discomfort all the host!

units.speaker: TROILUS
units.text:
You understand me not that tell me so:
I do not speak of flight, of fear, of death,
But dare all imminence that gods and men
Address their dangers in. Hector is gone:
Who shall tell Priam so, or Hecuba?
Let him that will a screech-owl aye be call'd,
Go in to Troy, and say there, Hector's dead:
There is a word will Priam turn to stone;
Make wells and Niobes of the maids and wives,
Cold statues of the youth, and, in a word,
Scare Troy out of itself. But, march away:
Hector is dead; there is no more to say.
Stay yet. You vile abominable tents,
Thus proudly pitched upon our Phrygian plains,
Let Titan rise as early as he dare,
I'll through and through you! and, thou great-sized
coward,
No space of earth shall sunder our two hates:
I'll haunt thee like a wicked conscience still,
That mouldeth goblins swift as frenzy's thoughts.
Strike a free march to Troy! with comfort go:
Hope of revenge shall hide our inward woe.
Exeunt AENEAS and Trojans
As TROILUS is going out, enter, from the other side, PANDARUS

units.speaker: PANDARUS
units.text: But hear you, hear you!

units.speaker: TROILUS
units.text:
Hence, broker-lackey! ignomy and shame
Pursue thy life, and live aye with thy name!
Exit

units.speaker: PANDARUS
units.text:
A goodly medicine for my aching bones! O world!
world! world! thus is the poor agent despised!
O traitors and bawds, how earnestly are you set
a-work, and how ill requited! why should our
endeavour be so loved and the performance so loathed?
what verse for it? what instance for it? Let me see:
Full merrily the humble-bee doth sing,
Till he hath lost his honey and his sting;
And being once subdued in armed tail,
Sweet honey and sweet notes together fail.
Good traders in the flesh, set this in your
painted cloths.
As many as be here of pander's hall,
Your eyes, half out, weep out at Pandar's fall;
Or if you cannot weep, yet give some groans,
Though not for me, yet for your aching bones.
Brethren and sisters of the hold-door trade,
Some two months hence my will shall here be made:
It should be now, but that my fear is this,
Some galled goose of Winchester would hiss:
Till then I'll sweat and seek about for eases,
And at that time bequeathe you my diseases.
Exit

sentences.speaker: PROLOGUE
sentences.text:
In Troy, there lies the scene. From isles of Greece
The princes orgulous, their high blood chafed,
Have to the port of Athens sent their ships,
Fraught with the ministers and instruments
Of cruel war: sixty and nine, that wore
Their crownets regal, from the Athenian bay
Put forth toward Phrygia; and their vow is made
To ransack Troy, within whose strong immures
The ravish'd Helen, Menelaus' queen,
With wanton Paris sleeps; and that's the quarrel.
To Tenedos they come;
And the deep-drawing barks do there disgorge
Their warlike fraughtage: now on Dardan plains
The fresh and yet unbruised Greeks do pitch
Their brave pavilions: Priam's six-gated city,
Dardan, and Tymbria, Helias, Chetas, Troien,
And Antenorides, with massy staples
And corresponsive and fulfilling bolts,
Sperr up the sons of Troy.
Now expectation, tickling skittish spirits,
On one and other side, Trojan and Greek,
Sets all on hazard: and hither am I come
A prologue arm'd, but not in confidence
Of author's pen or actor's voice, but suited
In like conditions as our argument,
To tell you, fair beholders, that our play
Leaps o'er the vaunt and firstlings of those broils,
Beginning in the middle, starting thence away
To what may be digested in a play.
Like or find fault; do as your pleasures are:
Now good or bad, 'tis but the chance of war.

Leaps o'er the vaunt and firstlings of those broils,
Beginning in the middle, starting thence away
To what may be digested in a play.
Like or find fault; do as your pleasures are:
Now good or bad, 'tis but the chance of war.

sentences.speaker: PROLOGUE
sentences.text:
In Troy, there lies the scene. From isles of Greece
The princes orgulous, their high blood chafed,
Have to the port of Athens sent their ships,
Fraught with the ministers and instruments
Of cruel war: sixty and nine, that wore
Their crownets regal, from the Athenian bay
Put forth toward Phrygia; and their vow is made
To ransack Troy, within whose strong immures
The ravish'd Helen, Menelaus' queen,
With wanton Paris sleeps; and that's the quarrel.
To Tenedos they come;
And the deep-drawing barks do there disgorge
Their warlike fraughtage: now on Dardan plains
The fresh and yet unbruised Greeks do pitch
Their brave pavilions: Priam's six-gated city,
Dardan, and Tymbria, Helias, Chetas, Troien,
And Antenorides, with massy staples
And corresponsive and fulfilling bolts,
Sperr up the sons of Troy.
Now expectation, tickling skittish spirits,
On one and other side, Trojan and Greek,
Sets all on hazard: and hither am I come
A prologue arm'd, but not in confidence
Of author's pen or actor's voice, but suited
In like conditions as our argument,
To tell you, fair beholders, that our play
Leaps o'er the vaunt and firstlings of those broils,
Beginning in the middle, starting thence away
To what may be digested in a play.
Like or find fault; do as your pleasures are:
Now good or bad, 'tis but the chance of war.

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text:
Call here my varlet; I'll unarm again:
Why should I war without the walls of Troy,
That find such cruel battle here within?
Each Trojan that is master of his heart,
Let him to field; Troilus, alas! hath none.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: Will this gear ne'er be mended?

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text:
The Greeks are strong and skilful to their strength,
Fierce to their skill and to their fierceness valiant;
But I am weaker than a woman's tear,
Tamer than sleep, fonder than ignorance,
Less valiant than the virgin in the night
And skilless as unpractised infancy.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Well, I have told you enough of this: for my part,
I'll not meddle nor make no further. He that will
have a cake out of the wheat must needs tarry the
grinding.

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text: Have I not tarried?

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Ay, the grinding; but you must tarry
the bolting.

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text: Have I not tarried?

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: Ay, the bolting, but you must tarry the leavening.

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text: Still have I tarried.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Ay, to the leavening; but here's yet in the word
'hereafter' the kneading, the making of the cake, the
heating of the oven and the baking; nay, you must
stay the cooling too, or you may chance to burn your
lips.

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text:
Patience herself, what goddess e'er she be,
Doth lesser blench at sufferance than I do.
At Priam's royal table do I sit;
And when fair Cressid comes into my thoughts,—
So, traitor! 'When she comes!' When is she thence?

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Well, she looked yesternight fairer than ever I saw
her look, or any woman else.

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text:
I was about to tell thee:— when my heart,
As wedged with a sigh, would rive in twain,
Lest Hector or my father should perceive me,
I have, as when the sun doth light a storm,
Buried this sigh in wrinkle of a smile:
But sorrow, that is couch'd in seeming gladness,
Is like that mirth fate turns to sudden sadness.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
An her hair were not somewhat darker than Helen's—
well, go to—there were no more comparison between
the women: but, for my part, she is my kinswoman; I
would not, as they term it, praise her: but I would
somebody had heard her talk yesterday, as I did. I
will not dispraise your sister Cassandra's wit, but—

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text:
O Pandarus! I tell thee, Pandarus,—
When I do tell thee, there my hopes lie drown'd,
Reply not in how many fathoms deep
They lie indrench'd. I tell thee I am mad
In Cressid's love: thou answer'st 'she is fair;'
Pour'st in the open ulcer of my heart
Her eyes, her hair, her cheek, her gait, her voice,
Handlest in thy discourse , O, that her hand,
In whose comparison all whites are ink,
Writing their own reproach, to whose soft seizure
The cygnet's down is harsh and spirit of sense
Hard as the palm of ploughman: this thou tell'st me,
As true thou tell'st me, when I say I love her;
But, saying thus, instead of oil and balm,
Thou lay'st in every gash that love hath given me
The knife that made it.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: I speak no more than truth.

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text: Thou dost not speak so much.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Faith, I'll not meddle in't. Let her be as she is:
if she be fair, 'tis the better for her; an she be
not, she has the mends in her own hands.

sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text: Good Pandarus, how now, Pandarus!

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
I have had my labour for my travail; ill-thought on of
her and ill-thought on of you; gone between and
between, but small thanks for my labour.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
I have had my labour for my travail; ill-thought on of
her and ill-thought on of you; gone between and
between, but small thanks for my labour.

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text: What, art thou angry, Pandarus? what, with me?

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text: What, art thou angry, Pandarus? what, with me?

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Because she's kin to me, therefore she's not so fair
as Helen: an she were not kin to me, she would be as
fair on Friday as Helen is on Sunday. But what care
I? I care not an she were a black-a-moor; 'tis all one
to me.

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text: Say I she is not fair?

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
I do not care whether you do or no. She's a fool to
stay behind her father; let her to the Greeks; and so
I'll tell her the next time I see her: for my part,
I'll meddle nor make no more i' the matter.

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text: Pandarus,—

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: Not I.

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text: Sweet Pandarus,—

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Pray you, speak no more to me: I will leave all as I
found it, and there an end.

Exit PANDARUS. An alarum

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text:
Peace, you ungracious clamours! peace, rude sounds!
Fools on both sides! Helen must needs be fair,
When with your blood you daily paint her thus.
I cannot fight upon this argument;
It is too starved a subject for my sword.
But Pandarus,—O gods, how do you plague me!
I cannot come to Cressid but by Pandar;
And he's as tetchy to be woo'd to woo.
As she is stubborn-chaste against all suit.
Tell me, Apollo, for thy Daphne's love,
What Cressid is, what Pandar, and what we?
Her bed is India; there she lies, a pearl:
Between our Ilium and where she resides,
Let it be call'd the wild and wandering flood,
Ourself the merchant, and this sailing Pandar
Our doubtful hope, our convoy and our bark.

What Cressid is, what Pandar, and what we?
Her bed is India; there she lies, a pearl:
Between our Ilium and where she resides,
Let it be call'd the wild and wandering flood,
Ourself the merchant, and this sailing Pandar
Our doubtful hope, our convoy and our bark.

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text:
Peace, you ungracious clamours! peace, rude sounds!
Fools on both sides! Helen must needs be fair,
When with your blood you daily paint her thus.
I cannot fight upon this argument;
It is too starved a subject for my sword.
But Pandarus,—O gods, how do you plague me!
I cannot come to Cressid but by Pandar;
And he's as tetchy to be woo'd to woo.
As she is stubborn-chaste against all suit.
Tell me, Apollo, for thy Daphne's love,
What Cressid is, what Pandar, and what we?
Her bed is India; there she lies, a pearl:
Between our Ilium and where she resides,
Let it be call'd the wild and wandering flood,
Ourself the merchant, and this sailing Pandar
Our doubtful hope, our convoy and our bark.

Alarum. Enter AENEAS
Alarum. Enter AENEAS

sentences.speaker: AENEAS
sentences.text: How now, Prince Troilus! wherefore not afield?

sentences.speaker: AENEAS
sentences.text: How now, Prince Troilus! wherefore not afield?

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text:
Because not there: this woman's answer sorts,
For womanish it is to be from thence.
What news, AEneas, from the field to-day?

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text:
Because not there: this woman's answer sorts,
For womanish it is to be from thence.
What news, AEneas, from the field to-day?

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text:
Because not there: this woman's answer sorts,
For womanish it is to be from thence.
What news, AEneas, from the field to-day?

sentences.speaker: AENEAS
sentences.text: That Paris is returned home and hurt.

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text: By whom, AEneas?

sentences.speaker: AENEAS
sentences.text: Troilus, by Menelaus.

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text:
Let Paris bleed; 'tis but a scar to scorn;
Paris is gored with Menelaus' horn.
Alarum

sentences.speaker: AENEAS
sentences.text: Hark, what good sport is out of town to-day!

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text:
Better at home, if 'would I might' were 'may.'
But to the sport abroad: are you bound thither?

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text:
Better at home, if 'would I might' were 'may.'
But to the sport abroad: are you bound thither?

sentences.speaker: AENEAS
sentences.text: In all swift haste.

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text: Come, go we then together.
Exeunt
Enter CRESSIDA and ALEXANDER

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: Who were those went by?

sentences.speaker: ALEXANDER
sentences.text: Queen Hecuba and Helen.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: And whither go they?

sentences.speaker: ALEXANDER
sentences.text:
Up to the eastern tower,
Whose height commands as subject all the vale,
To see the battle. Hector, whose patience
Is, as a virtue, fix'd, to-day was moved:
He chid Andromache and struck his armourer,
And, like as there were husbandry in war,
Before the sun rose he was harness'd light,
And to the field goes he; where every flower
Did, as a prophet, weep what it foresaw
In Hector's wrath.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: What was his cause of anger?

sentences.speaker: ALEXANDER
CRESSIDA
sentences.text: Good; and what of him?

sentences.speaker: ALEXANDER
sentences.text:
They say he is a very man per se,
And stands alone.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text:
So do all men, unless they are drunk, sick, or have no
legs.

sentences.speaker: ALEXANDER
sentences.text:
This man, lady, hath robbed many beasts of their
particular additions; he is as valiant as the lion,
churlish as the bear, slow as the elephant: a man
into whom nature hath so crowded humours that his
valour is crushed into folly, his folly sauced with
discretion: there is no man hath a virtue that he
hath not a glimpse of, nor any man an attaint but he
carries some stain of it: he is melancholy without
cause, and merry against the hair: he hath the
joints of every thing, but everything so out of joint
that he is a gouty Briareus, many hands and no use,
or purblind Argus, all eyes and no sight.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text:
But how should this man, that makes
me smile, make Hector angry?

sentences.speaker: ALEXANDER
sentences.text:
They say he yesterday coped Hector in the battle and
struck him down, the disdain and shame whereof hath
ever since kept Hector fasting and waking.

sentences.speaker: ALEXANDER
sentences.text:
They say he yesterday coped Hector in the battle and
struck him down, the disdain and shame whereof hath
ever since kept Hector fasting and waking.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: Who comes here?

sentences.speaker: ALEXANDER
sentences.text: Madam, your uncle Pandarus.
Enter PANDARUS

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: Hector's a gallant man.

sentences.speaker: ALEXANDER
sentences.text: As may be in the world, lady.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: What's that? what's that?

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: What's that? what's that?

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: Good morrow, uncle Pandarus.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Good morrow, cousin Cressid: what do you talk of?
Good morrow, Alexander. How do you, cousin? When
were you at Ilium?

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Good morrow, cousin Cressid: what do you talk of?
Good morrow, Alexander. How do you, cousin? When
were you at Ilium?

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Good morrow, cousin Cressid: what do you talk of?
Good morrow, Alexander. How do you, cousin? When
were you at Ilium?

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Good morrow, cousin Cressid: what do you talk of?
Good morrow, Alexander. How do you, cousin? When
were you at Ilium?

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: This morning, uncle.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
What were you talking of when I came? Was Hector
armed and gone ere ye came to Ilium? Helen was not
up, was she?

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
What were you talking of when I came? Was Hector
armed and gone ere ye came to Ilium? Helen was not
up, was she?

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
What were you talking of when I came? Was Hector
armed and gone ere ye came to Ilium? Helen was not
up, was she?

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: Hector was gone, but Helen was not up.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: Even so: Hector was stirring early.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: That were we talking of, and of his anger.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: Was he angry?

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: So he says here.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
True, he was so: I know the cause too: he'll lay
about him to-day, I can tell them that: and there's
Troilus will not come far behind him: let them take
heed of Troilus, I can tell them that too.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
True, he was so: I know the cause too: he'll lay
about him to-day, I can tell them that: and there's
Troilus will not come far behind him: let them take
heed of Troilus, I can tell them that too.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
True, he was so: I know the cause too: he'll lay

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: What, is he angry too?

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: What, is he angry too?

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: Who, Troilus? Troilus is the better man of the two.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: Who, Troilus? Troilus is the better man of the two.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: O Jupiter! there's no comparison.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
What, not between Troilus and Hector? Do you know a
man if you see him?

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
What, not between Troilus and Hector? Do you know a
man if you see him?

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
What, not between Troilus and Hector? Do you know a
man if you see him?

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: Ay, if I ever saw him before and knew him.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: Well, I say Troilus is Troilus.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text:
Then you say as I say; for, I am sure, he is not
Hector.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: No, nor Hector is not Troilus in some degrees.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: 'Tis just to each of them; he is himself.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: Himself! Alas, poor Troilus! I would he were.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: Himself! Alas, poor Troilus! I would he were.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: So he is.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: Condition, I had gone barefoot to India.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: He is not Hector.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Himself! no, he's not himself: would a' were
himself! Well, the gods are above; time must friend
or end: well, Troilus, well: I would my heart were
in her body. No, Hector is not a better man than
Troilus.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Himself! no, he's not himself: would a' were
himself! Well, the gods are above; time must friend

Troilus.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: Excuse me.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: He is elder.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: Pardon me, pardon me.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Th' other's not come to't; you shall tell me another
tale, when th' other's come to't. Hector shall not
have his wit this year.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Th' other's not come to't; you shall tell me another
tale, when th' other's come to't. Hector shall not
have his wit this year.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Th' other's not come to't; you shall tell me another
tale, when th' other's come to't. Hector shall not
have his wit this year.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: He shall not need it, if he have his own.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: Nor his qualities.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: No matter.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: Nor his beauty.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: 'Twould not become him; his own's better.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
You have no judgment, niece: Helen
herself swore th' other day, that Troilus, for
a brown favour—for so 'tis, I must confess,—
not brown neither,—

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
You have no judgment, niece: Helen
herself swore th' other day, that Troilus, for
a brown favour—for so 'tis, I must confess,—
not brown neither,—

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: No, but brown.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: 'Faith, to say truth, brown and not brown.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: To say the truth, true and not true.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: She praised his complexion above Paris.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: Why, Paris hath colour enough.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: So he has.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text:
Then Troilus should have too much: if she praised
him above, his complexion is higher than his; he
having colour enough, and the other higher, is too
flaming a praise for a good complexion. I had as
lief Helen's golden tongue had commended Troilus for
a copper nose.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text:
Then Troilus should have too much: if she praised
him above, his complexion is higher than his; he
having colour enough, and the other higher, is too
flaming a praise for a good complexion. I had as
lief Helen's golden tongue had commended Troilus for
a copper nose.

sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
I swear to you. I think Helen loves him better than
Paris.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: Then she's a merry Greek indeed.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Nay, I am sure she does. She came to him th' other
day into the compassed window,—and, you know, he
has not past three or four hairs on his chin,—

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Nay, I am sure she does. She came to him th' other
day into the compassed window,—and, you know, he
has not past three or four hairs on his chin,—

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text:
Indeed, a tapster's arithmetic may soon bring his
particulars therein to a total.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Why, he is very young: and yet will he, within
three pound, lift as much as his brother Hector.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: Is he so young a man and so old a lifter?

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
But to prove to you that Helen loves him: she came
and puts me her white hand to his cloven chin—

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: Juno have mercy! how came it cloven?

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: Juno have mercy! how came it cloven?

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Why, you know 'tis dimpled: I think his smiling
becomes him better than any man in all Phrygia.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Why, you know 'tis dimpled: I think his smiling
becomes him better than any man in all Phrygia.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: O, he smiles valiantly.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: Does he not?

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: O yes, an 'twere a cloud in autumn.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Why, go to, then: but to prove to you that Helen
loves Troilus,—

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text:
Troilus will stand to the proof, if you'll
prove it so.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Troilus! why, he esteems her no more than I esteem
an addle egg.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Troilus! why, he esteems her no more than I esteem
an addle egg.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text:
If you love an addle egg as well as you love an idle
head, you would eat chickens i' the shell.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
I cannot choose but laugh, to think how she tickled
his chin: indeed, she has a marvellous white hand, I
must needs confess,—

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
I cannot choose but laugh, to think how she tickled
his chin: indeed, she has a marvellous white hand, I
must needs confess,—

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: Without the rack.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: And she takes upon her to spy a white hair on his chin.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: Alas, poor chin! many a wart is richer.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
But there was such laughing! Queen Hecuba laughed
that her eyes ran o'er.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: With mill-stones.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: And Cassandra laughed.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text:
But there was more temperate fire under the pot of
her eyes: did her eyes run o'er too?

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: And Hector laughed.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: At what was all this laughing?

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Marry, at the white hair that Helen spied on Troilus'
chin.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text:
An't had been a green hair, I should have laughed
too.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
They laughed not so much at the hair as at his pretty
answer.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: What was his answer?

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Quoth she, 'Here's but two and fifty hairs on your
chin, and one of them is white.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: This is her question.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
That's true; make no question of that. 'Two and
fifty hairs' quoth he, 'and one white: that white
hair is my father, and all the rest are his sons.'
'Jupiter!' quoth she, 'which of these hairs is Paris,
my husband?' 'The forked one,' quoth he, 'pluck't
out, and give it him.' But there was such laughing!
and Helen so blushed, an Paris so chafed, and all the
rest so laughed, that it passed.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: So let it now; for it has been while going by.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: Well, cousin. I told you a thing yesterday; think on't.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: Well, cousin. I told you a thing yesterday; think on't.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: So I do.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
I'll be sworn 'tis true; he will weep you, an 'twere
a man born in April.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text:
And I'll spring up in his tears, an 'twere a nettle
against May.
A retreat sounded

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Hark! they are coming from the field: shall we
stand up here, and see them as they pass toward
Ilium? good niece, do, sweet niece Cressida.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS

sentences.text:

see most bravely: I'll tell you them all by their
names as they pass by; but mark Troilus above the rest.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: Who's that?

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
That's Antenor: he has a shrewd wit, I can tell you;
and he's a man good enough, he's one o' the soundest
judgments in whosoever, and a proper man of person.
When comes Troilus? I'll show you Troilus anon: if
he see me, you shall see him nod at me.

sentences.text:
That's Hector, that, that, look you, that; there's a
fellow ! Go thy way, Hector! There's a brave man,
niece. O brave Hector! Look how he looks! there's
a countenance! is't not a brave man?

sentences.text: O, a brave man!

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Is a' not? it does a man's heart good. Look you
what hacks are on his helmet! look you yonder, do
you see? look you there: there's no jesting;
there's laying on, take't off who will, as they say:
there be hacks!

to him, it's all one: by God's lid, it does one's
heart good. Yonder comes Paris, yonder comes Paris.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Look ye yonder, niece; is't not a gallant man too,
is't not? Why, this is brave now. Who said he came
hurt home to-day? he's not hurt: why, this will do
Helen's heart good now, ha! Would I could see
Troilus now! You shall see Troilus anon.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: Can Helenus fight, uncle?

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Helenus? no. Yes, he'll fight indifferent well. I
marvel where Troilus is. Hark! do you not hear the
people cry 'Troilus'? Helenus is a priest.

Where? yonder? that's Deiphobus. 'Tis Troilus!
there's a man, niece! Hem! Brave Troilus! the
prince of chivalry!

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: Peace, for shame, peace!

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Mark him; note him. O brave Troilus! Look well upon
him, niece: look you how his sword is bloodied, and
his helm more hacked than Hector's, and how he looks,
and how he goes! O admirable youth! he ne'er saw
three and twenty. Go thy way, Troilus, go thy way!
Had I a sister were a grace, or a daughter a goddess,
he should take his choice. O admirable man! Paris?
Paris is dirt to him; and, I warrant, Helen, to
change, would give an eye to boot.

his helm more hacked than Hector's, and how he looks,
and how he goes! O admirable youth! he ne'er saw
three and twenty. Go thy way, Troilus, go thy way!
Had I a sister were a grace, or a daughter a goddess,
he should take his choice. O admirable man! Paris?
Paris is dirt to him; and, I warrant, Helen, to
change, would give an eye to boot.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: Here come more.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: Here come more.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Asses, fools, dolts! chaff and bran, chaff and bran!
porridge after meat! I could live and die i' the
eyes of Troilus. Ne'er look, ne'er look: the eagles
are gone: crows and daws, crows and daws! I had
rather be such a man as Troilus than Agamemnon and
all Greece.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text:
There is among the Greeks Achilles, a better man than
Troilus.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: Achilles! a drayman, a porter, a very camel.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: Achilles! a drayman, a porter, a very camel.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: Well, well.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
'Well, well!' why, have you any discretion? have
you any eyes? Do you know what a man is? Is not
birth, beauty, good shape, discourse, manhood,
learning, gentleness, virtue, youth, liberality,
and such like, the spice and salt that season a man?

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text:
Ay, a minced man: and then to be baked with no date
in the pie, for then the man's date's out.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
You are such a woman! one knows not at what ward you
lie.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
You are such a woman! one knows not at what ward you
lie.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text:
Upon my back, to defend my belly; upon my wit, to
defend my wiles; upon my secrecy, to defend mine
honesty; my mask, to defend my beauty; and you, to
defend all these: and at all these wards I lie, at a
thousand watches.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text:
Upon my back, to defend my belly; upon my wit, to

honesty; my mask, to defend my beauty; and you, to

thousand watches.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: Say one of your watches.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text:
Nay, I'll watch you for that; and that's one of the
chiefest of them too: if I cannot ward what I would
not have hit, I can watch you for telling how I took
the blow; unless it swell past hiding, and then it's
past watching.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: You are such another!
Enter Troilus's Boy

sentences.speaker: BOY
sentences.text: Sir, my lord would instantly speak with you.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: Where?

sentences.speaker: BOY
sentences.text: At your own house; there he unarms him.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: Good boy, tell him I come.
Exit boy

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: I doubt he be hurt. Fare ye well, good niece.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: I doubt he be hurt. Fare ye well, good niece.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: Adieu, uncle.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: I'll be with you, niece, by and by.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: To bring, uncle?

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: Ay, a token from Troilus.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: By the same token, you are a bawd.
Exit PANDARUS

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text:
Words, vows, gifts, tears, and love's full sacrifice,
He offers in another's enterprise;
But more in Troilus thousand fold I see
Than in the glass of Pandar's praise may be;
Yet hold I off. Women are angels, wooing:
Things won are done; joy's soul lies in the doing.
That she beloved knows nought that knows not this:
Men prize the thing ungain'd more than it is:
That she was never yet that ever knew
Love got so sweet as when desire did sue.
Therefore this maxim out of love I teach:
Achievement is command; ungain'd, beseech:
Then though my heart's content firm love doth bear,
Nothing of that shall from mine eyes appear.

Sennet. Enter AGAMEMNON, NESTOR, ULYSSES, MENELAUS, and others

sentences.speaker: AGAMEMNON
sentences.text:
Princes,
What grief hath set the jaundice on your cheeks?
The ample proposition that hope makes
In all designs begun on earth below
Fails in the promised largeness: cheques and disasters
Grow in the veins of actions highest rear'd,
As knots, by the conflux of meeting sap,
Infect the sound pine and divert his grain
Tortive and errant from his course of growth.
Nor, princes, is it matter new to us
That we come short of our suppose so far
That after seven years' siege yet Troy walls stand;
Sith every action that hath gone before,
Whereof we have record, trial did draw
Bias and thwart, not answering the aim,
And that unbodied figure of the thought
That gave't surmised shape. Why then, you princes,
Do you with cheeks abash'd behold our works,
And call them shames? which are indeed nought else
But the protractive trials of great Jove
To find persistive constancy in men:
The fineness of which metal is not found
In fortune's love; for then the bold and coward,
The wise and fool, the artist and unread,
The hard and soft seem all affined and kin:
But, in the wind and tempest of her frown,
Distinction, with a broad and powerful fan,
Puffing at all, winnows the light away;
And what hath mass or matter, by itself
Lies rich in virtue and unmingled.

sentences.speaker: NESTOR
sentences.text:
With due observance of thy godlike seat,
Great Agamemnon, Nestor shall apply
Thy latest words. In the reproof of chance
Lies the true proof of men: the sea being smooth,
How many shallow bauble boats dare sail
Upon her patient breast, making their way
With those of nobler bulk!
But let the ruffian Boreas once enrage
The gentle Thetis, and anon behold
The strong-ribb'd bark through liquid mountains cut,
Bounding between the two moist elements,
Like Perseus' horse: where's then the saucy boat
Whose weak untimber'd sides but even now
Co-rivall'd greatness? Either to harbour fled,
Or made a toast for Neptune. Even so
Doth valour's show and valour's worth divide
In storms of fortune; for in her ray and brightness
The herd hath more annoyance by the breeze
Than by the tiger; but when the splitting wind
Makes flexible the knees of knotted oaks,
And flies fled under shade, why, then the thing of
courage
As roused with rage with rage doth sympathize,
And with an accent tuned in selfsame key
Retorts to chiding fortune.

Doth valour's show and valour's worth divide
In storms of fortune; for in her ray and brightness
The herd hath more annoyance by the breeze
Than by the tiger; but when the splitting wind
Makes flexible the knees of knotted oaks,
And flies fled under shade, why, then the thing of
courage
As roused with rage with rage doth sympathize,
And with an accent tuned in selfsame key
Retorts to chiding fortune.

sentences.speaker: NESTOR
sentences.text:
With due observance of thy godlike seat,
Great Agamemnon, Nestor shall apply
Thy latest words. In the reproof of chance
Lies the true proof of men: the sea being smooth,
How many shallow bauble boats dare sail
Upon her patient breast, making their way
With those of nobler bulk!
But let the ruffian Boreas once enrage
The gentle Thetis, and anon behold
The strong-ribb'd bark through liquid mountains cut,
Bounding between the two moist elements,
Like Perseus' horse: where's then the saucy boat
Whose weak untimber'd sides but even now
Co-rivall'd greatness? Either to harbour fled,
Or made a toast for Neptune. Even so
Doth valour's show and valour's worth divide
In storms of fortune; for in her ray and brightness
The herd hath more annoyance by the breeze
Than by the tiger; but when the splitting wind
Makes flexible the knees of knotted oaks,
And flies fled under shade, why, then the thing of
courage
As roused with rage with rage doth sympathize,
And with an accent tuned in selfsame key
Retorts to chiding fortune.

Agamemnon,
Thou great commander, nerve and bone of Greece,
Heart of our numbers, soul and only spirit.
In whom the tempers and the minds of all
Should be shut up , hear what Ulysses speaks.
Besides the applause and approbation To which,

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text: most mighty for thy place and sway,

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text: most mighty for thy place and sway,

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text:
And thou most reverend for thy stretch'd-out life
I give to both your speeches, which were such
As Agamemnon and the hand of Greece
Should hold up high in brass, and such again
As venerable Nestor, hatch'd in silver,
Should with a bond of air, strong as the axle-tree
On which heaven rides, knit all the Greekish ears
To his experienced tongue, yet let it please both,
Thou great, and wise, to hear Ulysses speak.

sentences.speaker: AGAMEMNON
sentences.text:
Speak, prince of Ithaca; and be't of less expect
That matter needless, of importless burden,
Divide thy lips, than we are confident,
When rank Thersites opes his mastic jaws,
We shall hear music, wit and oracle .

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text:
Troy, yet upon his basis, had been down,
And the great Hector's sword had lack'd a master,
But for these instances.
The specialty of rule hath been neglected:
And, look, how many Grecian tents do stand
Hollow upon this plain, so many hollow factions.
When that the general is not like the hive
To whom the foragers shall all repair,
What honey is expected? Degree being vizarded,
The unworthiest shows as fairly in the mask.
The heavens themselves, the planets and this centre
Observe degree, priority and place,
Insisture, course, proportion, season, form,
Office and custom, in all line of order;
And therefore is the glorious planet Sol
In noble eminence enthroned and sphered
Amidst the other; whose medicinable eye
Corrects the ill aspects of planets evil,
And posts, like the commandment of a king,
Sans cheque to good and bad: but when the planets
In evil mixture to disorder wander,
What plagues and what portents! what mutiny!
What raging of the sea! shaking of earth!
Commotion in the winds! frights, changes, horrors,
Divert and crack, rend and deracinate
The unity and married calm of states
Quite from their fixure! O, when degree is shaked,
Which is the ladder to all high designs,
Then enterprise is sick! How could communities,
Degrees in schools and brotherhoods in cities,
Peaceful commerce from dividable shores,
The primogenitive and due of birth,
Prerogative of age, crowns, sceptres, laurels,
But by degree, stand in authentic place?
Take but degree away, untune that string,
And, hark, what discord follows! each thing meets
In mere oppugnancy: the bounded waters
Should lift their bosoms higher than the shores
And make a sop of all this solid globe:
Strength should be lord of imbecility,
And the rude son should strike his father dead:
Force should be right; or rather, right and wrong,
Between whose endless jar justice resides,
Should lose their names, and so should justice too.
Then every thing includes itself in power,
Power into will, will into appetite;
And appetite, an universal wolf,
So doubly seconded with will and power,
Must make perforce an universal prey,
And last eat up himself. Great Agamemnon,
This chaos, when degree is suffocate,
Follows the choking.
And this neglection of degree it is
That by a pace goes backward, with a purpose
It hath to climb. The general's disdain'd
By him one step below, he by the next,
That next by him beneath; so every step,
Exampled by the first pace that is sick
Of his superior, grows to an envious fever
Of pale and bloodless emulation:
And 'tis this fever that keeps Troy on foot,
Not her own sinews. To end a tale of length,
Troy in our weakness stands, not in her strength.

It hath to climb. The general's disdain'd
By him one step below, he by the next,
That next by him beneath; so every step,
Exampled by the first pace that is sick
Of his superior, grows to an envious fever
Of pale and bloodless emulation:
And 'tis this fever that keeps Troy on foot,
Not her own sinews. To end a tale of length,
Troy in our weakness stands, not in her strength.

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text:
Troy, yet upon his basis, had been down,
And the great Hector's sword had lack'd a master,
But for these instances.
The specialty of rule hath been neglected:
And, look, how many Grecian tents do stand
Hollow upon this plain, so many hollow factions.
When that the general is not like the hive
To whom the foragers shall all repair,
What honey is expected? Degree being vizarded,
The unworthiest shows as fairly in the mask.
The heavens themselves, the planets and this centre
Observe degree, priority and place,
Insisture, course, proportion, season, form,
Office and custom, in all line of order;
And therefore is the glorious planet Sol
In noble eminence enthroned and sphered
Amidst the other; whose medicinable eye
Corrects the ill aspects of planets evil,
And posts, like the commandment of a king,
Sans cheque to good and bad: but when the planets
In evil mixture to disorder wander,
What plagues and what portents! what mutiny!
What raging of the sea! shaking of earth!
Commotion in the winds! frights, changes, horrors,
Divert and crack, rend and deracinate
The unity and married calm of states
Quite from their fixure! O, when degree is shaked,
Which is the ladder to all high designs,
Then enterprise is sick! How could communities,
Degrees in schools and brotherhoods in cities,
Peaceful commerce from dividable shores,
The primogenitive and due of birth,
Prerogative of age, crowns, sceptres, laurels,
But by degree, stand in authentic place?
Take but degree away, untune that string,
And, hark, what discord follows! each thing meets
In mere oppugnancy: the bounded waters
Should lift their bosoms higher than the shores
And make a sop of all this solid globe:
Strength should be lord of imbecility,
And the rude son should strike his father dead:
Force should be right; or rather, right and wrong,
Between whose endless jar justice resides,
Should lose their names, and so should justice too.
Then every thing includes itself in power,
Power into will, will into appetite;
And appetite, an universal wolf,
So doubly seconded with will and power,
Must make perforce an universal prey,
And last eat up himself. Great Agamemnon,
This chaos, when degree is suffocate,
Follows the choking.
And this neglection of degree it is
That by a pace goes backward, with a purpose
It hath to climb. The general's disdain'd
By him one step below, he by the next,
That next by him beneath; so every step,
Exampled by the first pace that is sick
Of his superior, grows to an envious fever
Of pale and bloodless emulation:
And 'tis this fever that keeps Troy on foot,
Not her own sinews. To end a tale of length,
Troy in our weakness stands, not in her strength.

sentences.speaker: NESTOR
sentences.text:
Most wisely hath Ulysses here discover'd
The fever whereof all our power is sick.

sentences.speaker: AGAMEMNON
sentences.text:
The nature of the sickness found, Ulysses,
What is the remedy?

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text:
The great Achilles, whom opinion crowns
The sinew and the forehand of our host,
Having his ear full of his airy fame,
Grows dainty of his worth, and in his tent
Lies mocking our designs: with him Patroclus
Upon a lazy bed the livelong day
Breaks scurril jests;
And with ridiculous and awkward action,
Which, slanderer, he imitation calls,
He pageants us. Sometime, great Agamemnon,
Thy topless deputation he puts on,
And, like a strutting player, whose conceit
Lies in his hamstring, and doth think it rich
To hear the wooden dialogue and sound
'Twixt his stretch'd footing and the scaffoldage,—
Such to-be-pitied and o'er-wrested seeming
He acts thy greatness in: and when he speaks,
'Tis like a chime a-mending; with terms unsquared,
Which, from the tongue of roaring Typhon dropp'd
Would seem hyperboles. At this fusty stuff
The large Achilles, on his press'd bed lolling,
From his deep chest laughs out a loud applause;
Cries 'Excellent! 'tis Agamemnon just.
Now play me Nestor; hem, and stroke thy beard,
As he being drest to some oration.'
That's done, as near as the extremest ends
Of parallels, as like as Vulcan and his wife:
Yet god Achilles still cries 'Excellent!
'Tis Nestor right. Now play him me, Patroclus,
Arming to answer in a night alarm.'
And then, forsooth, the faint defects of age
Must be the scene of mirth; to cough and spit,
And, with a palsy-fumbling on his gorget,
Shake in and out the rivet: and at this sport
Sir Valour dies; cries 'O, enough, Patroclus;
Or give me ribs of steel! I shall split all
In pleasure of my spleen.' And in this fashion,
All our abilities, gifts, natures, shapes,
Severals and generals of grace exact,
Achievements, plots, orders, preventions,
Excitements to the field, or speech for truce,
Success or loss, what is or is not, serves
As stuff for these two to make paradoxes.

sentences.speaker: NESTOR
sentences.text:
And in the imitation of these twain—
Who, as Ulysses says, opinion crowns
With an imperial voice—many are infect.
Ajax is grown self-will'd, and bears his head
In such a rein, in full as proud a place
As broad Achilles; keeps his tent like him;
Makes factious feasts; rails on our state of war,
Bold as an oracle, and sets Thersites,
A slave whose gall coins slanders like a mint,
To match us in comparisons with dirt,
To weaken and discredit our exposure,
How rank soever rounded in with danger.

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text:
They tax our policy, and call it cowardice,
Count wisdom as no member of the war,
Forestall prescience, and esteem no act
But that of hand: the still and mental parts,
That do contrive how many hands shall strike,
When fitness calls them on, and know by measure
Of their observant toil the enemies' weight,—
Why, this hath not a finger's dignity:
They call this bed-work, mappery, closet-war;
So that the ram that batters down the wall,
For the great swing and rudeness of his poise,
They place before his hand that made the engine,
Or those that with the fineness of their souls
By reason guide his execution.

They call this bed-work, mappery, closet-war;
So that the ram that batters down the wall,
For the great swing and rudeness of his poise,
They place before his hand that made the engine,
Or those that with the fineness of their souls
By reason guide his execution.

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text:
They tax our policy, and call it cowardice,
Count wisdom as no member of the war,
Forestall prescience, and esteem no act
But that of hand: the still and mental parts,
That do contrive how many hands shall strike,
When fitness calls them on, and know by measure
Of their observant toil the enemies' weight,—
Why, this hath not a finger's dignity:
They call this bed-work, mappery, closet-war;
So that the ram that batters down the wall,
For the great swing and rudeness of his poise,
They place before his hand that made the engine,
Or those that with the fineness of their souls
By reason guide his execution.

sentences.speaker: NESTOR
sentences.text:
Let this be granted, and Achilles' horse
Makes many Thetis' sons.
A tucket

sentences.speaker: AGAMEMNON
sentences.text: What trumpet? look, Menelaus.

sentences.speaker: AGAMEMNON
sentences.text: What trumpet? look, Menelaus.

sentences.speaker: AGAMEMNON
sentences.text: What trumpet? look, Menelaus.

sentences.speaker: MENELAUS
sentences.text: From Troy.
Enter AENEAS

sentences.speaker: AGAMEMNON
sentences.text: What would you 'fore our tent?

sentences.speaker: AENEAS
sentences.text: Is this great Agamemnon's tent, I pray you?

sentences.speaker: AGAMEMNON
sentences.text: Even this.

sentences.speaker: AENEAS
sentences.text:
May one, that is a herald and a prince,
Do a fair message to his kingly ears?

sentences.speaker: AGAMEMNON
sentences.text:
With surety stronger than Achilles' arm
'Fore all the Greekish heads, which with one voice
Call Agamemnon head and general.

sentences.speaker: AGAMEMNON
sentences.text:
With surety stronger than Achilles' arm
'Fore all the Greekish heads, which with one voice
Call Agamemnon head and general.

sentences.speaker: AENEAS
sentences.text:
Fair leave and large security. How may
A stranger to those most imperial looks
Know them from eyes of other mortals?

sentences.speaker: AENEAS
sentences.text:
Fair leave and large security. How may
A stranger to those most imperial looks
Know them from eyes of other mortals?

sentences.speaker: AGAMEMNON
sentences.text: How!

sentences.speaker: AENEAS
sentences.text:
Ay;
I ask, that I might waken reverence,
And bid the cheek be ready with a blush
Modest as morning when she coldly eyes
The youthful Phoebus:
Which is that god in office, guiding men?
Which is the high and mighty Agamemnon?

sentences.speaker: AENEAS
sentences.text:
Ay;
I ask, that I might waken reverence,
And bid the cheek be ready with a blush
Modest as morning when she coldly eyes
The youthful Phoebus:
Which is that god in office, guiding men?
Which is the high and mighty Agamemnon?

sentences.speaker: AGAMEMNON
sentences.text:
This Trojan scorns us; or the men of Troy
Are ceremonious courtiers.

sentences.speaker: AENEAS
sentences.text:
Courtiers as free, as debonair, unarm'd,
As bending angels; that's their fame in peace:
But when they would seem soldiers, they have galls,
Good arms, strong joints, true swords; and,
Jove's accord,
Nothing so full of heart. But peace, AEneas,
Peace, Trojan; lay thy finger on thy lips!
The worthiness of praise distains his worth,
If that the praised himself bring the praise forth:
But what the repining enemy commends,
That breath fame blows; that praise, sole sure,
transcends.

sentences.speaker: AGAMEMNON
sentences.text: Sir, you of Troy, call you yourself AEneas?

sentences.speaker: AENEAS
sentences.text: Ay, Greek, that is my name.

sentences.speaker: AGAMEMNON
sentences.text: What's your affair I pray you?

sentences.speaker: AENEAS
sentences.text: Sir, pardon; 'tis for Agamemnon's ears.

sentences.speaker: AGAMEMNON
sentences.text: He hears naught privately that comes from Troy.

sentences.speaker: AENEAS
sentences.text:
Nor I from Troy come not to whisper him:
I bring a trumpet to awake his ear,
To set his sense on the attentive bent,
And then to speak.

sentences.speaker: AGAMEMNON
sentences.text:
Speak frankly as the wind;
It is not Agamemnon's sleeping hour:
That thou shalt know. Trojan, he is awake,
He tells thee so himself.

sentences.speaker: AGAMEMNON
sentences.text:
Speak frankly as the wind;
It is not Agamemnon's sleeping hour:
That thou shalt know. Trojan, he is awake,
He tells thee so himself.

sentences.speaker: AENEAS
sentences.text:
Trumpet, blow loud,
Send thy brass voice through all these lazy tents;
And every Greek of mettle, let him know,
What Troy means fairly shall be spoke aloud.

sentences.speaker: AENEAS
sentences.text:
Trumpet, blow loud,
Send thy brass voice through all these lazy tents;
And every Greek of mettle, let him know,
What Troy means fairly shall be spoke aloud.
Trumpet sounds

sentences.speaker: AENEAS
sentences.text:
We have, great Agamemnon, here in Troy
A prince call'd Hector,—Priam is his father,—
Who in this dull and long-continued truce
Is rusty grown: he bade me take a trumpet,
And to this purpose speak. Kings, princes, lords!
If there be one among the fair'st of Greece
That holds his honour higher than his ease,
That seeks his praise more than he fears his peril,
That knows his valour, and knows not his fear,
That loves his mistress more than in confession,
With truant vows to her own lips he loves,
And dare avow her beauty and her worth
In other arms than hers,—to him this challenge.
Hector, in view of Trojans and of Greeks,
Shall make it good, or do his best to do it,
He hath a lady, wiser, fairer, truer,
Than ever Greek did compass in his arms,
And will to-morrow with his trumpet call
Midway between your tents and walls of Troy,
To rouse a Grecian that is true in love:
If any come, Hector shall honour him;
If none, he'll say in Troy when he retires,
The Grecian dames are sunburnt and not worth
The splinter of a lance. Even so much.

sentences.speaker: AGAMEMNON
sentences.text:
This shall be told our lovers, Lord AEneas;
If none of them have soul in such a kind,
We left them all at home: but we are soldiers;
And may that soldier a mere recreant prove,
That means not, hath not, or is not in love!
If then one is, or hath, or means to be,
That one meets Hector; if none else, I am he.

sentences.speaker: NESTOR
sentences.text:
Tell him of Nestor, one that was a man
When Hector's grandsire suck'd: he is old now;
But if there be not in our Grecian host
One noble man that hath one spark of fire,
To answer for his love, tell him from me
I'll hide my silver beard in a gold beaver
And in my vantbrace put this wither'd brawn,
And meeting him will tell him that my lady
Was fairer than his grandam and as chaste
As may be in the world: his youth in flood,
I'll prove this truth with my three drops of blood.

sentences.speaker: AENEAS
sentences.text: Now heavens forbid such scarcity of youth!

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text: Amen.

sentences.speaker: AGAMEMNON
AGAMEMNON
AGAMEMNON

sentences.text: Exeunt all but ULYSSES and NESTOR

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text: Nestor!

sentences.speaker: NESTOR
sentences.text: What says Ulysses?

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text:
I have a young conception in my brain;
Be you my time to bring it to some shape.

sentences.speaker: NESTOR
sentences.text: What is't?

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text:
This 'tis:
Blunt wedges rive hard knots: the seeded pride
That hath to this maturity blown up
In rank Achilles must or now be cropp'd,
Or, shedding, breed a nursery of like evil,
To overbulk us all.

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text:
This 'tis:
Blunt wedges rive hard knots: the seeded pride
That hath to this maturity blown up
In rank Achilles must or now be cropp'd,
Or, shedding, breed a nursery of like evil,
To overbulk us all.

sentences.speaker: NESTOR
sentences.text: Well, and how?

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text:
This challenge that the gallant Hector sends,
However it is spread in general name,
Relates in purpose only to Achilles.

sentences.speaker: NESTOR
sentences.text:
The purpose is perspicuous even as substance,
Whose grossness little characters sum up:
And, in the publication, make no strain,
But that Achilles, were his brain as barren
As banks of Libya,—though, Apollo knows,
'Tis dry enough,—will, with great speed of judgment,
Ay, with celerity, find Hector's purpose
Pointing on him.

sentences.speaker: NESTOR

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text: And wake him to the answer, think you?

sentences.speaker: NESTOR
sentences.text:
Yes, 'tis most meet: whom may you else oppose,
That can from Hector bring his honour off,
If not Achilles? Though't be a sportful combat,
Yet in the trial much opinion dwells;
For here the Trojans taste our dear'st repute
With their finest palate: and trust to me, Ulysses,
Our imputation shall be oddly poised
In this wild action; for the success,
Although particular, shall give a scantling
Of good or bad unto the general;
And in such indexes, although small pricks
To their subsequent volumes, there is seen
The baby figure of the giant mass
Of things to come at large. It is supposed
He that meets Hector issues from our choice
And choice, being mutual act of all our souls,
Makes merit her election, and doth boil,
As 'twere from us all, a man distill'd
Out of our virtues; who miscarrying,
What heart receives from hence the conquering part,
To steel a strong opinion to themselves?
Which entertain'd, limbs are his instruments,
In no less working than are swords and bows
Directive by the limbs.

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text:
Give pardon to my speech:
Therefore 'tis meet Achilles meet not Hector.
Let us, like merchants, show our foulest wares,
And think, perchance, they'll sell; if not,
The lustre of the better yet to show,
Shall show the better. Do not consent
That ever Hector and Achilles meet;
For both our honour and our shame in this
Are dogg'd with two strange followers.

sentences.speaker: NESTOR
sentences.text: I see them not with my old eyes: what are they?

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text:
What glory our Achilles shares from Hector,
Were he not proud, we all should share with him:
But he already is too insolent;
And we were better parch in Afric sun
Than in the pride and salt scorn of his eyes,
Should he 'scape Hector fair: if he were foil'd,
Why then, we did our main opinion crush
In taint of our best man. No, make a lottery;
And, by device, let blockish Ajax draw
The sort to fight with Hector: among ourselves
Give him allowance for the better man;
For that will physic the great Myrmidon
Who broils in loud applause, and make him fall
His crest that prouder than blue Iris bends.
If the dull brainless Ajax come safe off,
We'll dress him up in voices: if he fail,
Yet go we under our opinion still
That we have better men. But, hit or miss,
Our project's life this shape of sense assumes:
Ajax employ'd plucks down Achilles' plumes.

sentences.speaker: NESTOR
sentences.text:
Ulysses,
Now I begin to relish thy advice;
And I will give a taste of it forthwith
To Agamemnon: go we to him straight.
Two curs shall tame each other: pride alone
Must tarre the mastiffs on, as 'twere their bone.
