Enter AJAX and THERSITES

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text: Thersites!

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text:
Agamemnon, how if he had boils? full, all over,
generally?

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text: Thersites!

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text:
And those boils did run? say so: did not the
general run then? were not that a botchy core?

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text:
And those boils did run? say so: did not the
general run then? were not that a botchy core?

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text: Dog!

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text: Then would come some matter from him; I see none now.

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text: Thou bitch-wolf's son, canst thou not hear?

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text: Thou bitch-wolf's son, canst thou not hear?

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text: Feel, then.

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text:
The plague of Greece upon thee, thou mongrel
beef-witted lord!

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text:
Speak then, thou vinewedst leaven, speak: I will
beat thee into handsomeness.

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text:
I shall sooner rail thee into wit and holiness: but,
I think, thy horse will sooner con an oration than
thou learn a prayer without book . Thou canst strike,
canst thou? a red murrain o' thy jade's tricks!

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text:
I shall sooner rail thee into wit and holiness: but,
I think, thy horse will sooner con an oration than
thou learn a prayer without book . Thou canst strike,
canst thou? a red murrain o' thy jade's tricks!

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text:
I shall sooner rail thee into wit and holiness: but,
I think, thy horse will sooner con an oration than
thou learn a prayer without book . Thou canst strike,
canst thou? a red murrain o' thy jade's tricks!

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text: Toadstool, learn me the proclamation.

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text: Dost thou think I have no sense, thou strikest me thus?

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text: Dost thou think I have no sense, thou strikest me thus?

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text: The proclamation!

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text: Thou art proclaimed a fool, I think.

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text: Do not, porpentine, do not: my fingers itch.

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text:
I would thou didst itch from head to foot and I had
the scratching of thee; I would make thee the
loathsomest scab in Greece. When thou art forth in
the incursions, thou strikest as slow as another.

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text:
I would thou didst itch from head to foot and I had
the scratching of thee; I would make thee the
loathsomest scab in Greece. When thou art forth in
the incursions, thou strikest as slow as another.

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text: I say, the proclamation!

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text:
Thou grumblest and railest every hour on Achilles,
and thou art as full of envy at his greatness as
Cerberus is at Proserpine's beauty, ay, that thou
barkest at him.

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text: Mistress Thersites!

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text: Thou shouldest strike him.

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text: Cobloaf!

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text:
He would pun thee into shivers with his fist, as a
sailor breaks a biscuit.

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text: [Beating him] You whoreson cur!

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text: Do, do.

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text: Thou stool for a witch!

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text:
Ay, do, do; thou sodden-witted lord! thou hast no
more brain than I have in mine elbows; an assinego
may tutor thee: thou scurvy-valiant ass! thou art
here but to thrash Trojans; and thou art bought and
sold among those of any wit, like a barbarian slave.
If thou use to beat me, I will begin at thy heel, and
tell what thou art by inches, thou thing of no
bowels, thou!

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text: You dog!

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text: You scurvy lord!

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text: [Beating him] You cur!

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text: Mars his idiot! do, rudeness; do, camel; do, do.
Enter ACHILLES and PATROCLUS

sentences.speaker: ACHILLES
sentences.text:
Why, how now, Ajax! wherefore do you thus? How now,
Thersites! what's the matter, man?

sentences.speaker: ACHILLES
sentences.text:
Why, how now, Ajax! wherefore do you thus? How now,
Thersites! what's the matter, man?

sentences.speaker: ACHILLES
sentences.text:
Why, how now, Ajax! wherefore do you thus? How now,
Thersites! what's the matter, man?

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text: You see him there, do you?

sentences.speaker: ACHILLES
sentences.text: Ay; what's the matter?

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text: Nay, look upon him.

sentences.speaker: ACHILLES
sentences.text: So I do: what's the matter?

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text: Nay, but regard him well.

sentences.speaker: ACHILLES
sentences.text: 'Well!' why, I do so.

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text:
But yet you look not well upon him; for whosoever you
take him to be, he is Ajax.

sentences.speaker: ACHILLES
sentences.text: I know that, fool.

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text: Ay, but that fool knows not himself.

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text: Therefore I beat thee.

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text:
Lo, lo, lo, lo, what modicums of wit he utters! his
evasions have ears thus long. I have bobbed his
brain more than he has beat my bones: I will buy
nine sparrows for a penny, and his pia mater is not
worth the nineth part of a sparrow. This lord,
Achilles, Ajax, who wears his wit in his belly and
his guts in his head, I'll tell you what I say of
him.

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text:
Lo, lo, lo, lo, what modicums of wit he utters! his
evasions have ears thus long. I have bobbed his
brain more than he has beat my bones: I will buy
nine sparrows for a penny, and his pia mater is not
worth the nineth part of a sparrow. This lord,
Achilles, Ajax, who wears his wit in his belly and
his guts in his head, I'll tell you what I say of
him.

sentences.speaker: ACHILLES
sentences.text: What?

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text: I say, this Ajax—

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text: I say, this Ajax—

sentences.speaker: ACHILLES
sentences.text: Nay, good Ajax.

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text: Has not so much wit—

sentences.speaker: ACHILLES
sentences.text: Nay, I must hold you.

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text:
As will stop the eye of Helen's needle, for whom he
comes to fight.

sentences.speaker: ACHILLES
sentences.text: Peace, fool!

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text:
I would have peace and quietness, but the fool will
not: he there: that he: look you there.

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text: O thou damned cur! I shall—

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text: O thou damned cur! I shall—

sentences.speaker: ACHILLES
sentences.text: Will you set your wit to a fool's?

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text: No, I warrant you; for a fool's will shame it.

sentences.speaker: PATROCLUS
sentences.text: Good words, Thersites.

sentences.speaker: ACHILLES
sentences.text: What's the quarrel?

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text:
I bade the vile owl go learn me the tenor of the
proclamation, and he rails upon me.

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text: I serve thee not.

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text: Well, go to, go to.

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text: I serve here voluntarily.

sentences.speaker: ACHILLES
sentences.text:
Your last service was sufferance, 'twas not
voluntary: no man is beaten voluntary: Ajax was
here the voluntary, and you as under an impress.

sentences.speaker: ACHILLES
sentences.text:
Your last service was sufferance, 'twas not
voluntary: no man is beaten voluntary: Ajax was
here the voluntary, and you as under an impress.

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text:
E'en so; a great deal of your wit, too, lies in your
sinews, or else there be liars. Hector have a great
catch, if he knock out either of your brains: a'
were as good crack a fusty nut with no kernel.

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text:
E'en so; a great deal of your wit, too, lies in your
sinews, or else there be liars. Hector have a great
catch, if he knock out either of your brains: a'
were as good crack a fusty nut with no kernel.

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text:
E'en so; a great deal of your wit, too, lies in your
sinews, or else there be liars. Hector have a great
catch, if he knock out either of your brains: a'
were as good crack a fusty nut with no kernel.

sentences.speaker: ACHILLES
sentences.text: What, with me too, Thersites?

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text:
There's Ulysses and old Nestor, whose wit was mouldy
ere your grandsires had nails on their toes, yoke you
like draught-oxen and make you plough up the wars.

sentences.speaker: ACHILLES
sentences.text: What, what?

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text: Yes, good sooth: to, Achilles! to, Ajax! to!

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text: I shall cut out your tongue.

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text:
'Tis no matter! I shall speak as much as thou
afterwards.

sentences.speaker: PATROCLUS
sentences.text: No more words, Thersites; peace!

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text:
I will hold my peace when Achilles' brach bids me,
shall I?

sentences.speaker: ACHILLES
sentences.text: There's for you, Patroclus.

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text:
I will see you hanged, like clotpoles, ere I come
any more to your tents: I will keep where there is
wit stirring and leave the faction of fools.

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text:
I will see you hanged, like clotpoles, ere I come
any more to your tents: I will keep where there is
wit stirring and leave the faction of fools.
Exit

sentences.speaker: PATROCLUS
sentences.text: A good riddance.

sentences.speaker: ACHILLES
sentences.text:
Marry, this, sir, is proclaim'd through all our host:
That Hector, by the fifth hour of the sun,
Will with a trumpet 'twixt our tents and Troy
To-morrow morning call some knight to arms
That hath a stomach; and such a one that dare
Maintain—I know not what: 'tis trash. Farewell.

sentences.speaker: ACHILLES
sentences.text:
Marry, this, sir, is proclaim'd through all our host:
That Hector, by the fifth hour of the sun,
Will with a trumpet 'twixt our tents and Troy
To-morrow morning call some knight to arms
That hath a stomach; and such a one that dare
Maintain—I know not what: 'tis trash. Farewell.

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text: Farewell. Who shall answer him?

sentences.speaker: ACHILLES
sentences.text:
I know not: 'tis put to lottery; otherwise
He knew his man.

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text: O, meaning you. I will go learn more of it.

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text: O, meaning you. I will go learn more of it.
Exeunt
Enter PRIAM, HECTOR, TROILUS, PARIS, and HELENUS

sentences.speaker: PRIAM
sentences.text:
After so many hours, lives, speeches spent,
Thus once again says Nestor from the Greeks:
'Deliver Helen, and all damage else—
As honour, loss of time, travail, expense,
Wounds, friends, and what else dear that is consumed
In hot digestion of this cormorant war—
Shall be struck off.' Hector, what say you to't?

sentences.speaker: HECTOR
sentences.text:
Though no man lesser fears the Greeks than I
As far as toucheth my particular,
Yet, dread Priam,
There is no lady of more softer bowels,
More spongy to suck in the sense of fear,
More ready to cry out 'Who knows what follows?'
Than Hector is: the wound of peace is surety,
Surety secure; but modest doubt is call'd
The beacon of the wise, the tent that searches
To the bottom of the worst. Let Helen go:
Since the first sword was drawn about this question,
Every tithe soul, 'mongst many thousand dismes,
Hath been as dear as Helen; I mean, of ours:
If we have lost so many tenths of ours,
To guard a thing not ours nor worth to us,
Had it our name, the value of one ten,
What merit's in that reason which denies
The yielding of her up?

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text:
Fie, fie, my brother!
Weigh you the worth and honour of a king
So great as our dread father in a scale
Of common ounces? will you with counters sum
The past proportion of his infinite?
And buckle in a waist most fathomless
With spans and inches so diminutive
As fears and reasons? fie, for godly shame!

sentences.speaker: HELENUS
sentences.text:
No marvel, though you bite so sharp at reasons,
You are so empty of them. Should not our father
Bear the great sway of his affairs with reasons,
Because your speech hath none that tells him so?

sentences.speaker: HELENUS
sentences.text:

Because your speech hath none that tells him so?

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text:
You are for dreams and slumbers, brother priest;
You fur your gloves with reason. Here are
your reasons:
You know an enemy intends you harm;
You know a sword employ'd is perilous,
And reason flies the object of all harm:
Who marvels then, when Helenus beholds
A Grecian and his sword, if he do set
The very wings of reason to his heels
And fly like chidden Mercury from Jove,
Or like a star disorb'd? Nay, if we talk of reason,
Let's shut our gates and sleep: manhood and honour
Should have hare-hearts, would they but fat
their thoughts
With this cramm'd reason: reason and respect
Make livers pale and lustihood deject.

sentences.speaker: HECTOR
sentences.text:
Brother, she is not worth what she doth cost
The holding.

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text: What is aught, but as 'tis valued?

sentences.speaker: HECTOR
sentences.text:
But value dwells not in particular will;
It holds his estimate and dignity
As well wherein 'tis precious of itself
As in the prizer: 'tis mad idolatry
To make the service greater than the god
And the will dotes that is attributive
To what infectiously itself affects,
Without some image of the affected merit.

sentences.speaker: HECTOR
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text:
I take to-day a wife, and my election
Is led on in the conduct of my will;
My will enkindled by mine eyes and ears,
Two traded pilots 'twixt the dangerous shores
Of will and judgment: how may I avoid,
Although my will distaste what it elected,
The wife I chose? there can be no evasion
To blench from this and to stand firm by honour:
We turn not back the silks upon the merchant,
When we have soil'd them, nor the remainder viands
We do not throw in unrespective sieve,
Because we now are full. It was thought meet
Paris should do some vengeance on the Greeks:
Your breath of full consent bellied his sails;
The seas and winds, old wranglers, took a truce
And did him service: he touch'd the ports desired,
And for an old aunt whom the Greeks held captive,
He brought a Grecian queen, whose youth and freshness
Wrinkles Apollo's, and makes stale the morning.
Why keep we her? the Grecians keep our aunt:
Is she worth keeping? why, she is a pearl,
Whose price hath launch'd above a thousand ships,
And turn'd crown'd kings to merchants.
If you'll avouch 'twas wisdom Paris went—
As you must needs, for you all cried 'Go, go,'—
If you'll confess he brought home noble prize—
As you must needs, for you all clapp'd your hands
And cried 'Inestimable!'—why do you now
The issue of your proper wisdoms rate,
And do a deed that fortune never did,
Beggar the estimation which you prized
Richer than sea and land? O, theft most base,
That we have stol'n what we do fear to keep!
But, thieves, unworthy of a thing so stol'n,
That in their country did them that disgrace,
We fear to warrant in our native place!

Your breath of full consent bellied his sails;
The seas and winds, old wranglers, took a truce
And did him service: he touch'd the ports desired,
And for an old aunt whom the Greeks held captive,
He brought a Grecian queen, whose youth and freshness
Wrinkles Apollo's, and makes stale the morning.
Why keep we her? the Grecians keep our aunt:
Is she worth keeping? why, she is a pearl,
Whose price hath launch'd above a thousand ships,
And turn'd crown'd kings to merchants.
If you'll avouch 'twas wisdom Paris went—
As you must needs, for you all cried 'Go, go,'—
If you'll confess he brought home noble prize—
As you must needs, for you all clapp'd your hands
And cried 'Inestimable!'—why do you now
The issue of your proper wisdoms rate,
And do a deed that fortune never did,
Beggar the estimation which you prized
Richer than sea and land? O, theft most base,
That we have stol'n what we do fear to keep!
But, thieves, unworthy of a thing so stol'n,
That in their country did them that disgrace,
We fear to warrant in our native place!

sentences.speaker: CASSANDRA
sentences.text: [Within] Cry, Trojans, cry!

sentences.speaker: PRIAM
sentences.text: What noise? what shriek is this?

sentences.speaker: PRIAM
sentences.text: What noise? what shriek is this?

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text: 'Tis our mad sister, I do know her voice.

sentences.speaker: CASSANDRA
sentences.text: [Within] Cry, Trojans!

sentences.speaker: HECTOR
sentences.text: It is Cassandra.
Enter CASSANDRA, raving

sentences.speaker: CASSANDRA
sentences.text:
Cry, Trojans, cry! lend me ten thousand eyes,
And I will fill them with prophetic tears.

sentences.speaker: CASSANDRA
sentences.text:
Cry, Trojans, cry! lend me ten thousand eyes,
And I will fill them with prophetic tears.

sentences.speaker: HECTOR
sentences.text: Peace, sister, peace!

sentences.speaker: CASSANDRA
sentences.text:
Virgins and boys, mid-age and wrinkled eld,
Soft infancy, that nothing canst but cry,
Add to my clamours! let us pay betimes
A moiety of that mass of moan to come.
Cry, Trojans, cry! practise your eyes with tears!
Troy must not be, nor goodly Ilion stand;
Our firebrand brother, Paris, burns us all.
Cry, Trojans, cry! a Helen and a woe:
Cry, cry! Troy burns, or else let Helen go.

sentences.speaker: HECTOR
sentences.text:
Now, youthful Troilus, do not these high strains
Of divination in our sister work
Some touches of remorse? or is your blood
So madly hot that no discourse of reason,
Nor fear of bad success in a bad cause,
Can qualify the same?

sentences.speaker: HECTOR

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text:
Why, brother Hector,
We may not think the justness of each act
Such and no other than event doth form it,
Nor once deject the courage of our minds,
Because Cassandra's mad: her brain-sick raptures
Cannot distaste the goodness of a quarrel
Which hath our several honours all engaged
To make it gracious. For my private part,
I am no more touch'd than all Priam's sons:
And Jove forbid there should be done amongst us
Such things as might offend the weakest spleen
To fight for and maintain!

sentences.speaker: PARIS
sentences.text:
Else might the world convince of levity
As well my undertakings as your counsels:
But I attest the gods, your full consent
Gave wings to my propension and cut off
All fears attending on so dire a project.
For what, alas, can these my single arms?
What Propugnation is in one man's valour,
To stand the push and enmity of those
This quarrel would excite? Yet, I protest,
Were I alone to pass the difficulties
And had as ample power as I have will,
Paris should ne'er retract what he hath done,
Nor faint in the pursuit.

sentences.speaker: PRIAM
sentences.text:
Paris, you speak
Like one besotted on your sweet delights:
You have the honey still, but these the gall;
So to be valiant is no praise at all.

sentences.speaker: PRIAM
sentences.text:
Paris, you speak
Like one besotted on your sweet delights:
You have the honey still, but these the gall;
So to be valiant is no praise at all.

sentences.speaker: PARIS
sentences.text:
Sir, I propose not merely to myself
The pleasures such a beauty brings with it;
But I would have the soil of her fair rape
Wiped off, in honourable keeping her.
What treason were it to the ransack'd queen,
Disgrace to your great worths and shame to me,
Now to deliver her possession up
On terms of base compulsion! Can it be
That so degenerate a strain as this
Should once set footing in your generous bosoms?
There's not the meanest spirit on our party
Without a heart to dare or sword to draw
When Helen is defended, nor none so noble
Whose life were ill bestow'd or death unfamed
Where Helen is the subject; then, I say,
Well may we fight for her whom, we know well,
The world's large spaces cannot parallel.

sentences.speaker: HECTOR
sentences.text:
Paris and Troilus, you have both said well,
And on the cause and question now in hand
Have glozed, but superficially: not much
Unlike young men, whom Aristotle thought
Unfit to hear moral philosophy:
The reasons you allege do more conduce
To the hot passion of distemper'd blood
Than to make up a free determination
'Twixt right and wrong, for pleasure and revenge
Have ears more deaf than adders to the voice
Of any true decision. Nature craves
All dues be render'd to their owners: now,
What nearer debt in all humanity
Than wife is to the husband? If this law
Of nature be corrupted through affection,
And that great minds, of partial indulgence
To their benumbed wills, resist the same,
There is a law in each well-order'd nation
To curb those raging appetites that are
Most disobedient and refractory.
If Helen then be wife to Sparta's king,
As it is known she is, these moral laws
Of nature and of nations speak aloud
To have her back return'd: thus to persist
In doing wrong extenuates not wrong,
But makes it much more heavy. Hector's opinion
Is this in way of truth; yet ne'ertheless,
My spritely brethren, I propend to you
In resolution to keep Helen still,
For 'tis a cause that hath no mean dependance
Upon our joint and several dignities.

sentences.speaker: HECTOR
sentences.text:
Paris and Troilus, you have both said well,
And on the cause and question now in hand
Have glozed, but superficially: not much
Unlike young men, whom Aristotle thought
Unfit to hear moral philosophy:
The reasons you allege do more conduce
To the hot passion of distemper'd blood
Than to make up a free determination
'Twixt right and wrong, for pleasure and revenge
Have ears more deaf than adders to the voice
Of any true decision. Nature craves
All dues be render'd to their owners: now,
What nearer debt in all humanity
Than wife is to the husband? If this law
Of nature be corrupted through affection,
And that great minds, of partial indulgence
To their benumbed wills, resist the same,
There is a law in each well-order'd nation
To curb those raging appetites that are
Most disobedient and refractory.
If Helen then be wife to Sparta's king,
As it is known she is, these moral laws
Of nature and of nations speak aloud
To have her back return'd: thus to persist
In doing wrong extenuates not wrong,
But makes it much more heavy. Hector's opinion
Is this in way of truth; yet ne'ertheless,
My spritely brethren, I propend to you
In resolution to keep Helen still,
For 'tis a cause that hath no mean dependance
Upon our joint and several dignities.

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text:
Why, there you touch'd the life of our design:
Were it not glory that we more affected
Than the performance of our heaving spleens,
I would not wish a drop of Trojan blood
Spent more in her defence. But, worthy Hector,
She is a theme of honour and renown,
A spur to valiant and magnanimous deeds,
Whose present courage may beat down our foes,
And fame in time to come canonize us;
For, I presume, brave Hector would not lose
So rich advantage of a promised glory
As smiles upon the forehead of this action
For the wide world's revenue.

sentences.speaker: HECTOR
sentences.text:
I am yours,
You valiant offspring of great Priamus.
I have a roisting challenge sent amongst
The dun and factious nobles of the Greeks
Will strike amazement to their drowsy spirits:
I was advertised their great general slept,
Whilst emulation in the army crept:
This, I presume, will wake him.

Enter THERSITES, solus

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text:
How now, Thersites! what lost in the labyrinth of
thy fury! Shall the elephant Ajax carry it thus? He
beats me, and I rail at him: O, worthy satisfaction!
would it were otherwise; that I could beat him,
whilst he railed at me. 'Sfoot, I'll learn to
conjure and raise devils, but I'll see some issue of
my spiteful execrations. Then there's Achilles, a
rare enginer! If Troy be not taken till these two
undermine it, the walls will stand till they fall of
themselves. O thou great thunder-darter of Olympus,
forget that thou art Jove, the king of gods and,
Mercury, lose all the serpentine craft of thy
caduceus, if ye take not that little, little less
than little wit from them that they have! which
short-armed ignorance itself knows is so abundant
scarce, it will not in circumvention deliver a fly
from a spider, without drawing their massy irons and
cutting the web. After this, the vengeance on the
whole camp! or rather, the bone-ache! for that,
methinks, is the curse dependent on those that war
for a placket. I have said my prayers and devil Envy
say Amen. What ho! my Lord Achilles!

Enter PATROCLUS

sentences.speaker: PATROCLUS
sentences.text:
Who's there? Thersites! Good Thersites, come in and
rail.

sentences.speaker: PATROCLUS
sentences.text:
Who's there? Thersites! Good Thersites, come in and
rail.

sentences.speaker: PATROCLUS
sentences.text:
Who's there? Thersites! Good Thersites, come in and
rail.

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text:
If I could have remembered a gilt counterfeit, thou
wouldst not have slipped out of my contemplation: but
it is no matter; thyself upon thyself! The common
curse of mankind, folly and ignorance, be thine in
great revenue! heaven bless thee from a tutor, and
discipline come not near thee! Let thy blood be thy
direction till thy death! then if she that lays thee
out says thou art a fair corse, I'll be sworn and
sworn upon't she never shrouded any but lazars.
Amen. Where's Achilles?

wouldst not have slipped out of my contemplation: but
it is no matter; thyself upon thyself! The common
curse of mankind, folly and ignorance, be thine in
great revenue! heaven bless thee from a tutor, and
discipline come not near thee! Let thy blood be thy
direction till thy death! then if she that lays thee
out says thou art a fair corse, I'll be sworn and
sworn upon't she never shrouded any but lazars.
Amen. Where's Achilles?

sentences.speaker: PATROCLUS
sentences.text: What, art thou devout? wast thou in prayer?

sentences.speaker: PATROCLUS
sentences.text: What, art thou devout? wast thou in prayer?

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text: Ay: the heavens hear me!
Enter ACHILLES

sentences.speaker: ACHILLES
sentences.text: Who's there?

sentences.speaker: PATROCLUS
sentences.text: Thersites, my lord.

sentences.speaker: ACHILLES
sentences.text:
Where, where? Art thou come? why, my cheese, my
digestion, why hast thou not served thyself in to
my table so many meals? Come, what's Agamemnon?

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text:

what art thou?

sentences.speaker: PATROCLUS
sentences.text: Thou mayst tell that knowest.

sentences.speaker: ACHILLES
sentences.text: O, tell, tell.

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text:
I'll decline the whole question. Agamemnon commands
Achilles; Achilles is my lord; I am Patroclus'
knower, and Patroclus is a fool.

sentences.speaker: THERSITES

sentences.speaker: PATROCLUS

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text: Peace, fool! I have not done.

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text: Peace, fool! I have not done.

sentences.speaker: ACHILLES

sentences.speaker: ACHILLES

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text:
Agamemnon is a fool; Achilles is a fool; Thersites
is a fool, and, as aforesaid, Patroclus is a fool.

sentences.speaker: ACHILLES
sentences.text: Derive this; come.

sentences.speaker: THERSITES

PATROCLUS
sentences.text: Why am I a fool?

sentences.text:
Make that demand of the prover. It suffices me thou
art. Look you, who comes here?

sentences.speaker: THERSITES

sentences.speaker: ACHILLES
sentences.text:
Patroclus, I'll speak with nobody.
Come in with me, Thersites.
Exit

sentences.speaker: THERSITES
sentences.text:
Here is such patchery, such juggling and such
knavery! all the argument is a cuckold and a
whore; a good quarrel to draw emulous factions
and bleed to death upon. Now, the dry serpigo on
the subject! and war and lechery confound all!

Enter AGAMEMNON, ULYSSES, NESTOR, DIOMEDES, and AJAX

sentences.speaker: AGAMEMNON
sentences.text: Where is Achilles?

sentences.speaker: PATROCLUS
sentences.text: Within his tent; but ill disposed, my lord.

sentences.speaker: AGAMEMNON
sentences.text:
Let it be known to him that we are here.
He shent our messengers; and we lay by
Our appertainments, visiting of him:
Let him be told so; lest perchance he think
We dare not move the question of our place,
Or know not what we are.

sentences.speaker: AGAMEMNON

sentences.speaker: PATROCLUS
sentences.text: I shall say so to him.
Exit

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text:
We saw him at the opening of his tent:
He is not sick.

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text:
Yes, lion-sick, sick of proud heart: you may call it
melancholy, if you will favour the man; but, by my
head, 'tis pride: but why, why? let him show us the
cause. A word, my lord.

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text:
Yes, lion-sick, sick of proud heart: you may call it
melancholy, if you will favour the man; but, by my
head, 'tis pride: but why, why? let him show us the
cause. A word, my lord.

sentences.speaker: NESTOR
sentences.text: What moves Ajax thus to bay at him?

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text: Achilles hath inveigled his fool from him.

sentences.speaker: NESTOR
sentences.text: Who, Thersites?

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text: He.

sentences.speaker: NESTOR
sentences.text:
Then will Ajax lack matter, if he have lost his
argument.

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text:
No, you see, he is his argument that has his
argument, Achilles.

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text:
No, you see, he is his argument that has his
argument, Achilles.

sentences.speaker: NESTOR
sentences.text:
All the better; their fraction is more our wish than
their faction: but it was a strong composure a fool
could disunite.

sentences.speaker: NESTOR
sentences.text:
All the better; their fraction is more our wish than
their faction: but it was a strong composure a fool
could disunite.

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text:
The amity that wisdom knits not, folly may easily
untie. Here comes Patroclus.

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text:
The amity that wisdom knits not, folly may easily
untie. Here comes Patroclus.
Re-enter PATROCLUS

sentences.speaker: NESTOR
sentences.text: No Achilles with him.

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text:
The elephant hath joints, but none for courtesy:
his legs are legs for necessity, not for flexure.

sentences.speaker: PATROCLUS
sentences.text:
Achilles bids me say, he is much sorry,
If any thing more than your sport and pleasure
Did move your greatness and this noble state
To call upon him; he hopes it is no other
But for your health and your digestion sake,
And after-dinner's breath.

sentences.speaker: PATROCLUS
sentences.text:
Achilles bids me say, he is much sorry,
If any thing more than your sport and pleasure
Did move your greatness and this noble state
To call upon him; he hopes it is no other
But for your health and your digestion sake,
And after-dinner's breath.

sentences.speaker: AGAMEMNON
sentences.text:
Hear you, Patroclus:
We are too well acquainted with these answers:
But his evasion, wing'd thus swift with scorn,
Cannot outfly our apprehensions.
Much attribute he hath, and much the reason
Why we ascribe it to him; yet all his virtues,
Not virtuously on his own part beheld,
Do in our eyes begin to lose their gloss,
Yea, like fair fruit in an unwholesome dish,
Are like to rot untasted. Go and tell him,
We come to speak with him; and you shall not sin,
If you do say we think him over-proud
And under-honest, in self-assumption greater
Than in the note of judgment; and worthier
than himself
Here tend the savage strangeness he puts on,
Disguise the holy strength of their command,
And underwrite in an observing kind
His humorous predominance; yea, watch
His pettish lunes, his ebbs, his flows, as if
The passage and whole carriage of this action
Rode on his tide. Go tell him this, and add,
That if he overhold his price so much,
We'll none of him; but let him, like an engine
Not portable, lie under this report:
'Bring action hither, this cannot go to war:
A stirring dwarf we do allowance give
Before a sleeping giant.' Tell him so.

sentences.speaker: PATROCLUS
sentences.text: I shall; and bring his answer presently.
Exit

sentences.speaker: AGAMEMNON
sentences.text:
In second voice we'll not be satisfied;
We come to speak with him. Ulysses, enter you.

sentences.speaker: AGAMEMNON
sentences.text:
In second voice we'll not be satisfied;
We come to speak with him. Ulysses, enter you.
Exit ULYSSES

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text: What is he more than another?

sentences.speaker: AGAMEMNON
sentences.text: No more than what he thinks he is.

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text:
Is he so much? Do you not think he thinks himself a
better man than I am?

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text:
Is he so much? Do you not think he thinks himself a
better man than I am?

sentences.speaker: AGAMEMNON
sentences.text: No question.

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text: Will you subscribe his thought, and say he is?

sentences.speaker: AGAMEMNON
sentences.text:
No, noble Ajax; you are as strong, as valiant, as
wise, no less noble, much more gentle, and altogether
more tractable.

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text:
Why should a man be proud? How doth pride grow? I
know not what pride is.

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text:
Why should a man be proud? How doth pride grow? I
know not what pride is.

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text:
Why should a man be proud? How doth pride grow? I
know not what pride is.

sentences.speaker: AGAMEMNON
sentences.text:
Your mind is the clearer, Ajax, and your virtues the
fairer. He that is proud eats up himself: pride is
his own glass, his own trumpet, his own chronicle;
and whatever praises itself but in the deed, devours
the deed in the praise.

the deed in the praise.

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text:
I do hate a proud man, as I hate the engendering of
toads.

sentences.speaker: NESTOR
sentences.text: [Aside] Yet he loves himself: is't not strange?
Re-enter ULYSSES

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text: Achilles will not to the field to-morrow.

sentences.speaker: AGAMEMNON
sentences.text: What's his excuse?

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text:
He doth rely on none,
But carries on the stream of his dispose
Without observance or respect of any,
In will peculiar and in self-admission.

sentences.speaker: AGAMEMNON
sentences.text:
Why will he not upon our fair request
Untent his person and share the air with us?

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text:
Things small as nothing, for request's sake only,
He makes important: possess'd he is with greatness,
And speaks not to himself but with a pride
That quarrels at self-breath: imagined worth
Holds in his blood such swoln and hot discourse
That 'twixt his mental and his active parts
Kingdom'd Achilles in commotion rages
And batters down himself: what should I say?
He is so plaguy proud that the death-tokens of it
Cry 'No recovery.'

sentences.speaker: AGAMEMNON
sentences.text:
Let Ajax go to him.
Dear lord, go you and greet him in his tent:
'Tis said he holds you well, and will be led
At your request a little from himself.

Dear lord, go you and greet him in his tent:
'Tis said he holds you well, and will be led
At your request a little from himself.

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text:
O Agamemnon, let it not be so!
We'll consecrate the steps that Ajax makes
When they go from Achilles: shall the proud lord
That bastes his arrogance with his own seam
And never suffers matter of the world
Enter his thoughts, save such as do revolve
And ruminate himself, shall he be worshipp'd
Of that we hold an idol more than he?
No, this thrice worthy and right valiant lord
Must not so stale his palm, nobly acquired;
Nor, by my will, assubjugate his merit,
As amply titled as Achilles is,
By going to Achilles:
That were to enlard his fat already pride
And add more coals to Cancer when he burns
With entertaining great Hyperion.
This lord go to him! Jupiter forbid,
And say in thunder 'Achilles go to him.'

sentences.text:
[Aside to DIOMEDES] O, this is well; he rubs the
vein of him.

sentences.speaker: DIOMEDES
sentences.text:
[Aside to NESTOR] And how his silence drinks up
this applause!

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text:
If I go to him, with my armed fist I'll pash him o'er
the face.

sentences.speaker: AGAMEMNON
sentences.text: O, no, you shall not go.

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text:
An a' be proud with me, I'll pheeze his pride:
Let me go to him.

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text:
An a' be proud with me, I'll pheeze his pride:
Let me go to him.

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text: Not for the worth that hangs upon our quarrel.

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text: A paltry, insolent fellow!

sentences.speaker: NESTOR
sentences.text: How he describes himself!

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text: Can he not be sociable?

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text: The raven chides blackness.

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text: I'll let his humours blood.

sentences.speaker: AGAMEMNON
sentences.text: He will be the physician that should be the patient.

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text: An all men were o' my mind,—

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text: Wit would be out of fashion.

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text:
A' should not bear it so, a' should eat swords first:
shall pride carry it?

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text:
A' should not bear it so, a' should eat swords first:
shall pride carry it?

sentences.speaker: NESTOR
sentences.text: An 'twould, you'ld carry half.

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text: A' would have ten shares.

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text: I will knead him; I'll make him supple.

sentences.speaker: NESTOR
sentences.text:
He's not yet through warm: force him with praises:
pour in, pour in; his ambition is dry.

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text:
[To AGAMEMNON] My lord, you feed too much on this
dislike.

sentences.speaker: NESTOR
sentences.text: Our noble general, do not do so.

sentences.speaker: DIOMEDES
sentences.text: You must prepare to fight without Achilles.

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
ULYSSES
NESTOR
sentences.text:
Wherefore should you so?
He is not emulous, as Achilles is.

sentences.speaker: NESTOR
sentences.text:
Wherefore should you so?
He is not emulous, as Achilles is.

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text: Know the whole world, he is as valiant.

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text:
A whoreson dog, that shall pelter thus with us!
Would he were a Trojan!

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text:

Would he were a Trojan!

sentences.speaker: NESTOR
sentences.text: What a vice were it in Ajax now,—

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text: If he were proud,—

sentences.speaker: DIOMEDES
sentences.text: Or covetous of praise,—

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text: Ay, or surly borne,—

sentences.speaker: DIOMEDES
sentences.text: Or strange, or self-affected!

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text:
Thank the heavens, lord, thou art of sweet composure;
Praise him that got thee, she that gave thee suck:
Famed be thy tutor, and thy parts of nature
Thrice famed, beyond all erudition:
But he that disciplined thy arms to fight,
Let Mars divide eternity in twain,
And give him half: and, for thy vigour,
Bull-bearing Milo his addition yield
To sinewy Ajax. I will not praise thy wisdom,
Which, like a bourn, a pale, a shore, confines
Thy spacious and dilated parts: here's Nestor;
Instructed by the antiquary times,
He must, he is, he cannot but be wise:
Put pardon, father Nestor, were your days
As green as Ajax' and your brain so temper'd,
You should not have the eminence of him,
But be as Ajax.

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text: Shall I call you father?

sentences.speaker: NESTOR
sentences.text: Ay, my good son.

sentences.speaker: DIOMEDES
sentences.text: Be ruled by him, Lord Ajax.

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text:
There is no tarrying here; the hart Achilles
Keeps thicket . Please it our great general
To call together all his state of war;
Fresh kings are come to Troy: to-morrow
We must with all our main of power stand fast:
And here's a lord,—come knights from east to west,
And cull their flower, Ajax shall cope the best.

sentences.speaker: AGAMEMNON
sentences.text:
Go we to council. Let Achilles sleep:
Light boats sail swift, though greater hulks draw deep.

sentences.speaker: AGAMEMNON

Enter a Servant and PANDARUS

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Friend, you! pray you, a word: do not you follow
the young Lord Paris?

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Friend, you! pray you, a word: do not you follow
the young Lord Paris?

sentences.speaker: SERVANT
sentences.text: Ay, sir, when he goes before me.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: You depend upon him, I mean?

sentences.speaker: SERVANT
sentences.text: Sir, I do depend upon the lord.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
You depend upon a noble gentleman; I must needs
praise him.

sentences.speaker: SERVANT
sentences.text: The lord be praised!

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: You know me, do you not?

sentences.speaker: SERVANT
sentences.text: Faith, sir, superficially.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: Friend, know me better; I am the Lord Pandarus.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: Friend, know me better; I am the Lord Pandarus.

sentences.speaker: SERVANT
sentences.text: I hope I shall know your honour better.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: I do desire it.

sentences.speaker: SERVANT
sentences.text: You are in the state of grace.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Grace! not so, friend: honour and lordship are my
titles.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Grace! not so, friend: honour and lordship are my
titles.
Music within

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: What music is this?

sentences.speaker: SERVANT
sentences.text: I do but partly know, sir: it is music in parts.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: Know you the musicians?

sentences.speaker: SERVANT
sentences.text: Wholly, sir.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: Who play they to?

sentences.speaker: SERVANT
sentences.text: To the hearers, sir.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: At whose pleasure, friend

sentences.speaker: SERVANT
sentences.text: At mine, sir, and theirs that love music.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: Command, I mean, friend.

sentences.speaker: SERVANT
sentences.text: Who shall I command, sir?

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Friend, we understand not one another: I am too
courtly and thou art too cunning. At whose request
do these men play?

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Friend, we understand not one another: I am too

sentences.speaker: SERVANT
sentences.text:
That's to 't indeed, sir: marry, sir, at the request
of Paris my lord, who's there in person; with him,
the mortal Venus, the heart-blood of beauty, love's
invisible soul,—

sentences.speaker: SERVANT

No, sir, Helen: could you not find out that by her
attributes?

It should seem, fellow, that thou hast not seen the
Lady Cressida. I come to speak with Paris from the
Prince Troilus: I will make a complimental assault
upon him, for my business seethes.

Enter PARIS and HELEN, attended

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Fair be to you, my lord, and to all this fair
company! fair desires, in all fair measure,
fairly guide them! especially to you, fair queen!
fair thoughts be your fair pillow!

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:

company! fair desires, in all fair measure,
fairly guide them! especially to you, fair queen!

sentences.speaker: HELEN

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS

You speak your fair pleasure, sweet queen. Fair
prince, here is good broken music.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS

You have broke it, cousin: and, by my life, you
shall make it whole again; you shall piece it out
with a piece of your performance. Nell, he is full
of harmony.

with you. You must not know where he sups.

No, no, no such matter; you are wide: come, your
disposer is sick.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: PARIS

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:

your poor disposer's sick.

sentences.text:
You spy! what do you spy? Come, give me an
instrument. Now, sweet queen.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
You spy! what do you spy? Come, give me an

sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
You spy! what do you spy? Come, give me an
instrument. Now, sweet queen.

sentences.speaker: HELEN
sentences.text: Why, this is kindly done.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
My niece is horribly in love with a thing you have,
sweet queen.

sentences.speaker: HELEN
sentences.text: She shall have it, my lord, if it be not my lord Paris.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: He! no, she'll none of him; they two are twain.

sentences.speaker: HELEN
sentences.text: Falling in, after falling out, may make them three.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Come, come, I'll hear no more of this; I'll sing
you a song now.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Come, come, I'll hear no more of this; I'll sing
you a song now.

sentences.speaker: HELEN
sentences.text:
Ay, ay, prithee now. By my troth, sweet lord, thou
hast a fine forehead.

sentences.speaker: HELEN
sentences.text:
Ay, ay, prithee now. By my troth, sweet lord, thou
hast a fine forehead.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: Ay, you may, you may.

sentences.speaker: HELEN
sentences.text:
Let thy song be love: this love will undo us all.
O Cupid, Cupid, Cupid!

sentences.speaker: HELEN
sentences.text:
Let thy song be love: this love will undo us all.
O Cupid, Cupid, Cupid!

sentences.speaker: HELEN
sentences.text:
Let thy song be love: this love will undo us all.
O Cupid, Cupid, Cupid!

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: Love! ay, that it shall, i' faith.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: Love! ay, that it shall, i' faith.

sentences.speaker: PARIS
sentences.text: Ay, good now, love, love, nothing but love.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: In good troth, it begins so.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: In good troth, it begins so.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Love, love, nothing but love, still more!
For, O, love's bow
Shoots buck and doe:
The shaft confounds,
Not that it wounds,
But tickles still the sore.
These lovers cry Oh! oh! they die!
Yet that which seems the wound to kill,
Doth turn oh! oh! to ha! ha! he!
So dying love lives still:
Oh! oh! a while, but ha! ha! ha!
Oh! oh! groans out for ha! ha! ha!
Heigh-ho!

sentences.speaker: HELEN
sentences.text: In love, i' faith, to the very tip of the nose.

sentences.speaker: PARIS
sentences.text:
He eats nothing but doves, love, and that breeds hot
blood. Hot blood begets hot thoughts, and hot
thoughts beget hot deeds, and hot deeds is love.

sentences.speaker: PARIS
sentences.text:

thoughts beget hot deeds, and hot deeds is love.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Is this the generation of love? hot blood, hot
thoughts, and hot deeds? Why, they are vipers:
is love a generation of vipers? Sweet lord, who's
a-field to-day?

sentences.speaker: PARIS
sentences.text:
Hector, Deiphobus, Helenus, Antenor, and all the
gallantry of Troy: I would fain have armed to-day,
but my Nell would not have it so. How chance my
brother Troilus went not?

sentences.speaker: HELEN

He hangs the lip at something: you know all, Lord
Pandarus.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Not I, honey-sweet queen. I long to hear how they
sped to-day. You'll remember your brother's excuse?

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS

sentences.speaker: PARIS
sentences.text: To a hair.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: Farewell, sweet queen.

sentences.speaker: HELEN
sentences.text: Commend me to your niece.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: I will, sweet queen.
Exit
A retreat sounded

sentences.speaker: PARIS
sentences.text:
They're come from field: let us to Priam's hall,
To greet the warriors. Sweet Helen, I must woo you
To help unarm our Hector: his stubborn buckles,
With these your white enchanting fingers touch'd,
Shall more obey than to the edge of steel
Or force of Greekish sinews; you shall do more
Than all the island kings,—disarm great Hector.

'Twill make us proud to be his servant, Paris;
Yea, what he shall receive of us in duty
Gives us more palm in beauty than we have,
Yea, overshines ourself.

sentences.text: Sweet, above thought I love thee.
Exeunt
Enter PANDARUS and Troilus's Boy, meeting

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
How now! where's thy master? at my cousin
Cressida's?

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
How now! where's thy master? at my cousin
Cressida's?

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
How now! where's thy master? at my cousin
Cressida's?

sentences.speaker: BOY
sentences.text: No, sir; he stays for you to conduct him thither.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: O, here he comes.
Enter TROILUS

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: How now, how now!

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text: Sirrah, walk off.
Exit Boy

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: Have you seen my cousin?

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text:
No, Pandarus: I stalk about her door,
Like a strange soul upon the Stygian banks
Staying for waftage. O, be thou my Charon,
And give me swift transportance to those fields
Where I may wallow in the lily-beds
Proposed for the deserver! O gentle Pandarus,
From Cupid's shoulder pluck his painted wings
And fly with me to Cressid!

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: Walk here i' the orchard, I'll bring her straight.
Exit

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text:
I am giddy; expectation whirls me round.
The imaginary relish is so sweet
That it enchants my sense: what will it be,
When that the watery palate tastes indeed
Love's thrice repured nectar? death, I fear me,
Swooning destruction, or some joy too fine,
Too subtle-potent, tuned too sharp in sweetness,
For the capacity of my ruder powers:
I fear it much; and I do fear besides,
That I shall lose distinction in my joys;
As doth a battle, when they charge on heaps
The enemy flying.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
She's making her ready, she'll come straight: you
must be witty now. She does so blush, and fetches
her wind so short, as if she were frayed with a
sprite: I'll fetch her. It is the prettiest
villain: she fetches her breath as short as a
new-ta'en sparrow.

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text:
Even such a passion doth embrace my bosom:
My heart beats thicker than a feverous pulse;
And all my powers do their bestowing lose,
Like vassalage at unawares encountering
The eye of majesty.

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text:

Like vassalage at unawares encountering
The eye of majesty.
Re-enter PANDARUS with CRESSIDA

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Come, come, what need you blush? shame's a baby.
Here she is now: swear the oaths now to her that
you have sworn to me. What, are you gone again?
you must be watched ere you be made tame, must you?
Come your ways, come your ways; an you draw backward,
we'll put you i' the fills. Why do you not speak to
her? Come, draw this curtain, and let's see your
picture. Alas the day, how loath you are to offend
daylight! an 'twere dark, you'ld close sooner.
So, so; rub on, and kiss the mistress. How now!
a kiss in fee-farm! build there, carpenter; the air
is sweet. Nay, you shall fight your hearts out ere
I part you. The falcon as the tercel, for all the
ducks i' the river: go to, go to.

sentences.text: You have bereft me of all words, lady.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Words pay no debts, give her deeds: but she'll
bereave you o' the deeds too, if she call your
activity in question. What, billing again? Here's
'In witness whereof the parties interchangeably'—
Come in, come in: I'll go get a fire.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS

Exit

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: Will you walk in, my lord?

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text: O Cressida, how often have I wished me thus!

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: Wished, my lord! The gods grant,—O my lord!

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: Wished, my lord! The gods grant,—O my lord!

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text:
What should they grant? what makes this pretty
abruption? What too curious dreg espies my sweet
lady in the fountain of our love?

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text:
What should they grant? what makes this pretty
abruption? What too curious dreg espies my sweet
lady in the fountain of our love?

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text:

lady in the fountain of our love?

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text:
What should they grant? what makes this pretty
abruption? What too curious dreg espies my sweet
lady in the fountain of our love?

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: More dregs than water, if my fears have eyes.

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text: Fears make devils of cherubims; they never see truly.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text:
Blind fear, that seeing reason leads, finds safer
footing than blind reason stumbling without fear: to
fear the worst oft cures the worse.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text:
Blind fear, that seeing reason leads, finds safer
footing than blind reason stumbling without fear: to
fear the worst oft cures the worse.

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text:
O, let my lady apprehend no fear: in all Cupid's
pageant there is presented no monster.

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text:
O, let my lady apprehend no fear: in all Cupid's
pageant there is presented no monster.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: Nor nothing monstrous neither?

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text:
Nothing, but our undertakings; when we vow to weep
seas, live in fire, eat rocks, tame tigers; thinking
it harder for our mistress to devise imposition
enough than for us to undergo any difficulty imposed.
This is the monstruosity in love, lady, that the will
is infinite and the execution confined, that the
desire is boundless and the act a slave to limit.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text:
They say all lovers swear more performance than they
are able and yet reserve an ability that they never
perform, vowing more than the perfection often and
discharging less than the tenth part of one. They
that have the voice of lions and the act of hares,
are they not monsters?

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text:
Are there such? such are not we: praise us as we
are tasted, allow us as we prove; our head shall go
bare till merit crown it: no perfection in reversion
shall have a praise in present: we will not name
desert before his birth, and, being born, his addition
shall be humble. Few words to fair faith: Troilus
shall be such to Cressid as what envy can say worst
shall be a mock for his truth, and what truth can
speak truest not truer than Troilus.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: Will you walk in, my lord?
Re-enter PANDARUS

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: What, blushing still? have you not done talking yet?

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: What, blushing still? have you not done talking yet?

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: Well, uncle, what folly I commit, I dedicate to you.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
I thank you for that: if my lord get a boy of you,
you'll give him me. Be true to my lord: if he
flinch, chide me for it.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
I thank you for that: if my lord get a boy of you,
you'll give him me. Be true to my lord: if he
flinch, chide me for it.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
I thank you for that: if my lord get a boy of you,
you'll give him me. Be true to my lord: if he
flinch, chide me for it.

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text:
You know now your hostages; your uncle's word and my
firm faith.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Nay, I'll give my word for her too: our kindred,
though they be long ere they are wooed, they are
constant being won: they are burs, I can tell you;
they'll stick where they are thrown.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text:
Boldness comes to me now, and brings me heart.
Prince Troilus, I have loved you night and day
For many weary months.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text:
Boldness comes to me now, and brings me heart.
Prince Troilus, I have loved you night and day
For many weary months.

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text: Why was my Cressid then so hard to win?

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text:
Hard to seem won: but I was won, my lord,
With the first glance that ever—pardon me—
If I confess much, you will play the tyrant.
I love you now; but not, till now, so much
But I might master it: in faith, I lie;
My thoughts were like unbridled children, grown
Too headstrong for their mother. See, we fools!
Why have I blabb'd? who shall be true to us,
When we are so unsecret to ourselves?
But, though I loved you well, I woo'd you not;
And yet, good faith, I wish'd myself a man,
Or that we women had men's privilege
Of speaking first. Sweet, bid me hold my tongue,
For in this rapture I shall surely speak
The thing I shall repent. See, see, your silence,
Cunning in dumbness, from my weakness draws
My very soul of counsel! stop my mouth.

sentences.text: And shall, albeit sweet music issues thence.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text: Pretty, i' faith.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text:
My lord, I do beseech you, pardon me;
'Twas not my purpose, thus to beg a kiss:
I am ashamed. O heavens! what have I done?
For this time will I take my leave, my lord.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text: What offends you, lady?

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: Sir, mine own company.

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text: You cannot shun yourself.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text:
Let me go and try:
I have a kind of self resides with you;
But an unkind self, that itself will leave,
To be another's fool. I would be gone:
Where is my wit? I know not what I speak.

To be another's fool. I would be gone:
Where is my wit? I know not what I speak.

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text: Well know they what they speak that speak so wisely.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text:
Perchance, my lord, I show more craft than love;
And fell so roundly to a large confession,
To angle for your thoughts: but you are wise,
Or else you love not, for to be wise and love
Exceeds man's might; that dwells with gods above.

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text:
O that I thought it could be in a woman—
As, if it can, I will presume in you—
To feed for aye her lamp and flames of love;
To keep her constancy in plight and youth,
Outliving beauty's outward, with a mind
That doth renew swifter than blood decays!
Or that persuasion could but thus convince me,
That my integrity and truth to you
Might be affronted with the match and weight
Of such a winnow'd purity in love;
How were I then uplifted! but, alas!
I am as true as truth's simplicity
And simpler than the infancy of truth.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: In that I'll war with you.

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text:
O virtuous fight,
When right with right wars who shall be most right!
True swains in love shall in the world to come
Approve their truths by Troilus: when their rhymes,
Full of protest, of oath and big compare,
Want similes, truth tired with iteration,
As true as steel, as plantage to the moon,
As sun to day, as turtle to her mate,
As iron to adamant, as earth to the centre,
Yet, after all comparisons of truth,
As truth's authentic author to be cited,
'As true as Troilus' shall crown up the verse,
And sanctify the numbers.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text:
Prophet may you be!
If I be false, or swerve a hair from truth,
When time is old and hath forgot itself,
When waterdrops have worn the stones of Troy,
And blind oblivion swallow'd cities up,
And mighty states characterless are grated
To dusty nothing, yet let memory,
From false to false, among false maids in love,
Upbraid my falsehood! when they've said 'as false
As air, as water, wind, or sandy earth,
As fox to lamb, as wolf to heifer's calf,
Pard to the hind, or stepdame to her son,'
'Yea,' let them say, to stick the heart of falsehood,
'As false as Cressid.'

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Go to, a bargain made: seal it, seal it; I'll be the
witness. Here I hold your hand, here my cousin's.
If ever you prove false one to another, since I have
taken such pains to bring you together, let all
pitiful goers-between be called to the world's end
after my name; call them all Pandars; let all
constant men be Troiluses, all false women Cressids,
and all brokers-between Pandars! say, amen.

sentences.speaker: TROILUS
sentences.text: Amen.

sentences.speaker: CRESSIDA
sentences.text: Amen.

sentences.speaker: PANDARUS
sentences.text:
Amen. Whereupon I will show you a chamber with a
bed; which bed, because it shall not speak of your
pretty encounters, press it to death: away!
And Cupid grant all tongue-tied maidens here
Bed, chamber, Pandar to provide this gear!

Enter AGAMEMNON, ULYSSES, DIOMEDES, NESTOR, AJAX, MENELAUS, and CALCHAS

sentences.speaker: CALCHAS
sentences.text:
Now, princes, for the service I have done you,
The advantage of the time prompts me aloud
To call for recompense. Appear it to your mind
That, through the sight I bear in things to love,
I have abandon'd Troy, left my possession,
Incurr'd a traitor's name; exposed myself,
From certain and possess'd conveniences,
To doubtful fortunes; sequestering from me all
That time, acquaintance, custom and condition
Made tame and most familiar to my nature,
And here, to do you service, am become
As new into the world, strange, unacquainted:
I do beseech you, as in way of taste,
To give me now a little benefit,
Out of those many register'd in promise,
Which, you say, live to come in my behalf.

sentences.speaker: AGAMEMNON
sentences.text: What wouldst thou of us, Trojan? make demand.

sentences.speaker: AGAMEMNON
sentences.text: What wouldst thou of us, Trojan? make demand.

sentences.speaker: CALCHAS
sentences.text:
You have a Trojan prisoner, call'd Antenor,
Yesterday took: Troy holds him very dear.
Oft have you—often have you thanks therefore—
Desired my Cressid in right great exchange,
Whom Troy hath still denied: but this Antenor,
I know, is such a wrest in their affairs
That their negotiations all must slack,
Wanting his manage; and they will almost
Give us a prince of blood, a son of Priam,
In change of him: let him be sent, great princes,
And he shall buy my daughter; and her presence
Shall quite strike off all service I have done,
In most accepted pain.

sentences.speaker: AGAMEMNON
sentences.text:
Let Diomedes bear him,
And bring us Cressid hither: Calchas shall have
What he requests of us. Good Diomed,
Furnish you fairly for this interchange:
Withal bring word if Hector will to-morrow
Be answer'd in his challenge: Ajax is ready.

sentences.speaker: DIOMEDES
sentences.text:
This shall I undertake; and 'tis a burden
Which I am proud to bear.
Exeunt DIOMEDES and CALCHAS
Enter ACHILLES and PATROCLUS, before their tent

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text:
Achilles stands i' the entrance of his tent:
Please it our general to pass strangely by him,
As if he were forgot; and, princes all,
Lay negligent and loose regard upon him:
I will come last. 'Tis like he'll question me
Why such unplausive eyes are bent on him:
If so, I have derision medicinable,
To use between your strangeness and his pride,
Which his own will shall have desire to drink:
It may be good: pride hath no other glass
To show itself but pride, for supple knees
Feed arrogance and are the proud man's fees.

sentences.speaker: AGAMEMNON
sentences.text:
We'll execute your purpose, and put on
A form of strangeness as we pass along:
So do each lord, and either greet him not,
Or else disdainfully, which shall shake him more
Than if not look'd on. I will lead the way.

sentences.speaker: ACHILLES
sentences.text:
What, comes the general to speak with me?
You know my mind, I'll fight no more 'gainst Troy.

sentences.speaker: AGAMEMNON
sentences.text: What says Achilles? would he aught with us?

sentences.speaker: AGAMEMNON
sentences.text: What says Achilles? would he aught with us?

sentences.speaker: NESTOR
sentences.text: Would you, my lord, aught with the general?

sentences.speaker: ACHILLES
sentences.text: No.

sentences.speaker: NESTOR
sentences.text: Nothing, my lord.

sentences.speaker: AGAMEMNON
sentences.text: The better.
Exeunt AGAMEMNON and NESTOR

sentences.speaker: ACHILLES
sentences.text: Good day, good day.

sentences.speaker: MENELAUS
sentences.text: How do you? how do you?

sentences.speaker: MENELAUS
sentences.text: How do you? how do you?
Exit

sentences.speaker: ACHILLES
sentences.text: What, does the cuckold scorn me?

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text: How now, Patroclus!

sentences.speaker: ACHILLES
sentences.text: Good morrow, Ajax.

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text: Ha?

sentences.speaker: ACHILLES
sentences.text: Good morrow.

sentences.speaker: AJAX
sentences.text: Ay, and good next day too.
Exit

sentences.speaker: ACHILLES
sentences.text: What mean these fellows? Know they not Achilles?

sentences.speaker: ACHILLES
sentences.text: What mean these fellows? Know they not Achilles?

sentences.speaker: PATROCLUS
sentences.text:
They pass by strangely: they were used to bend
To send their smiles before them to Achilles;
To come as humbly as they used to creep
To holy altars.

sentences.speaker: PATROCLUS
sentences.text:
They pass by strangely: they were used to bend
To send their smiles before them to Achilles;
To come as humbly as they used to creep
To holy altars.

sentences.speaker: ACHILLES
sentences.text:
What, am I poor of late?
'Tis certain, greatness, once fall'n out with fortune,
Must fall out with men too: what the declined is
He shall as soon read in the eyes of others
As feel in his own fall; for men, like butterflies,
Show not their mealy wings but to the summer,
And not a man, for being simply man,
Hath any honour, but honour for those honours
That are without him, as place, riches, favour,
Prizes of accident as oft as merit:
Which when they fall, as being slippery standers,
The love that lean'd on them as slippery too,
Do one pluck down another and together
Die in the fall. But 'tis not so with me:
Fortune and I are friends: I do enjoy
At ample point all that I did possess,
Save these men's looks; who do, methinks, find out
Something not worth in me such rich beholding
As they have often given. Here is Ulysses;
I'll interrupt his reading.
How now Ulysses!

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text: Now, great Thetis' son!

sentences.speaker: ACHILLES
sentences.text: What are you reading?

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text:
A strange fellow here
Writes me: 'That man, how dearly ever parted,
How much in having, or without or in,
Cannot make boast to have that which he hath,
Nor feels not what he owes, but by reflection;
As when his virtues shining upon others
Heat them and they retort that heat again
To the first giver.'

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text:
A strange fellow here
Writes me: 'That man, how dearly ever parted,
How much in having, or without or in,
Cannot make boast to have that which he hath,
Nor feels not what he owes, but by reflection;
As when his virtues shining upon others
Heat them and they retort that heat again
To the first giver.'

sentences.speaker: ACHILLES
sentences.text:
This is not strange, Ulysses.
The beauty that is borne here in the face
The bearer knows not, but commends itself
To others' eyes; nor doth the eye itself,
That most pure spirit of sense, behold itself,
Not going from itself; but eye to eye opposed
Salutes each other with each other's form;
For speculation turns not to itself,
Till it hath travell'd and is mirror'd there
Where it may see itself. This is not strange at all.

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text:
I do not strain at the position,—
It is familiar,—but at the author's drift;
Who, in his circumstance, expressly proves
That no man is the lord of any thing,
Though in and of him there be much consisting,
Till he communicate his parts to others:
Nor doth he of himself know them for aught
Till he behold them form'd in the applause
Where they're extended; who, like an arch,
reverberates
The voice again, or, like a gate of steel
Fronting the sun, receives and renders back
His figure and his heat. I was much wrapt in this;
And apprehended here immediately
The unknown Ajax.
Heavens, what a man is there! a very horse,
That has he knows not what. Nature, what things there
are
Most abject in regard and dear in use!
What things again most dear in the esteem
And poor in worth! Now shall we see to-morrow—
An act that very chance doth throw upon him—
Ajax renown'd. O heavens, what some men do,
While some men leave to do!
How some men creep in skittish fortune's hall,
Whiles others play the idiots in her eyes!
How one man eats into another's pride,
While pride is fasting in his wantonness!
To see these Grecian lords!— why, even already
They clap the lubber Ajax on the shoulder,
As if his foot were on brave Hector's breast
And great Troy shrieking.

That has he knows not what. Nature, what things there
are
Most abject in regard and dear in use!
What things again most dear in the esteem
And poor in worth! Now shall we see to-morrow—
An act that very chance doth throw upon him—
Ajax renown'd. O heavens, what some men do,
While some men leave to do!
How some men creep in skittish fortune's hall,
Whiles others play the idiots in her eyes!
How one man eats into another's pride,
While pride is fasting in his wantonness!
To see these Grecian lords!— why, even already
They clap the lubber Ajax on the shoulder,
As if his foot were on brave Hector's breast
And great Troy shrieking.

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text:
I do not strain at the position,—

Who, in his circumstance, expressly proves
That no man is the lord of any thing,
Though in and of him there be much consisting,
Till he communicate his parts to others:
Nor doth he of himself know them for aught
Till he behold them form'd in the applause
Where they're extended; who, like an arch,
reverberates
The voice again, or, like a gate of steel
Fronting the sun, receives and renders back
His figure and his heat. I was much wrapt in this;
And apprehended here immediately
The unknown Ajax.

sentences.speaker: ACHILLES
sentences.text:
I do believe it; for they pass'd by me
As misers do by beggars, neither gave to me
Good word nor look: what, are my deeds forgot?

sentences.speaker: ACHILLES
sentences.text:
I do believe it; for they pass'd by me
As misers do by beggars, neither gave to me
Good word nor look: what, are my deeds forgot?

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text:
Time hath, my lord, a wallet at his back,
Wherein he puts alms for oblivion,
A great-sized monster of ingratitudes:
Those scraps are good deeds past; which are devour'd
As fast as they are made, forgot as soon
As done: perseverance, dear my lord,
Keeps honour bright: to have done is to hang
Quite out of fashion, like a rusty mail
In monumental mockery. Take the instant way;
For honour travels in a strait so narrow,
Where one but goes abreast: keep then the path;
For emulation hath a thousand sons
That one by one pursue: if you give way,
Or hedge aside from the direct forthright,
Like to an enter'd tide, they all rush by
And leave you hindmost;
Or like a gallant horse fall'n in first rank,
Lie there for pavement to the abject rear,
O'er-run and trampled on: then what they do in present,
Though less than yours in past, must o'ertop yours;
For time is like a fashionable host
That slightly shakes his parting guest by the hand,
And with his arms outstretch'd, as he would fly,
Grasps in the comer: welcome ever smiles,
And farewell goes out sighing. O, let not
virtue seek
Remuneration for the thing it was;
For beauty, wit,
High birth, vigour of bone, desert in service,
Love, friendship, charity, are subjects all
To envious and calumniating time.
One touch of nature makes the whole world kin,
That all with one consent praise new-born gawds,
Though they are made and moulded of things past,
And give to dust that is a little gilt
More laud than gilt o'er-dusted.
The present eye praises the present object.
Then marvel not, thou great and complete man,
That all the Greeks begin to worship Ajax;
Since things in motion sooner catch the eye
Than what not stirs. The cry went once on thee,
And still it might, and yet it may again,
If thou wouldst not entomb thyself alive
And case thy reputation in thy tent;
Whose glorious deeds, but in these fields of late,
Made emulous missions 'mongst the gods themselves
And drave great Mars to faction.

That slightly shakes his parting guest by the hand,
And with his arms outstretch'd, as he would fly,
Grasps in the comer: welcome ever smiles,
And farewell goes out sighing. O, let not
virtue seek
Remuneration for the thing it was;
For beauty, wit,
High birth, vigour of bone, desert in service,
Love, friendship, charity, are subjects all
To envious and calumniating time.
One touch of nature makes the whole world kin,
That all with one consent praise new-born gawds,
Though they are made and moulded of things past,
And give to dust that is a little gilt
More laud than gilt o'er-dusted.
The present eye praises the present object.
Then marvel not, thou great and complete man,
That all the Greeks begin to worship Ajax;
Since things in motion sooner catch the eye
Than what not stirs. The cry went once on thee,
And still it might, and yet it may again,
If thou wouldst not entomb thyself alive
And case thy reputation in thy tent;
Whose glorious deeds, but in these fields of late,
Made emulous missions 'mongst the gods themselves
And drave great Mars to faction.

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text:
Time hath, my lord, a wallet at his back,
Wherein he puts alms for oblivion,
A great-sized monster of ingratitudes:
Those scraps are good deeds past; which are devour'd
As fast as they are made, forgot as soon
As done: perseverance, dear my lord,
Keeps honour bright: to have done is to hang
Quite out of fashion, like a rusty mail
In monumental mockery. Take the instant way;
For honour travels in a strait so narrow,
Where one but goes abreast: keep then the path;
For emulation hath a thousand sons
That one by one pursue: if you give way,
Or hedge aside from the direct forthright,
Like to an enter'd tide, they all rush by
And leave you hindmost;
Or like a gallant horse fall'n in first rank,
Lie there for pavement to the abject rear,
O'er-run and trampled on: then what they do in present,
Though less than yours in past, must o'ertop yours;
For time is like a fashionable host
That slightly shakes his parting guest by the hand,
And with his arms outstretch'd, as he would fly,
Grasps in the comer: welcome ever smiles,
And farewell goes out sighing. O, let not
virtue seek

sentences.speaker: ACHILLES
sentences.text:
Of this my privacy
I have strong reasons.

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text:
But 'gainst your privacy
The reasons are more potent and heroical:
'Tis known, Achilles, that you are in love
With one of Priam's daughters.

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text:
But 'gainst your privacy
The reasons are more potent and heroical:
'Tis known, Achilles, that you are in love
With one of Priam's daughters.

sentences.speaker: ACHILLES
sentences.text: Ha! known!

sentences.speaker: ULYSSES
sentences.text:
Is that a wonder?
The providence that's in a watchful state
Knows almost every grain of Plutus' gold,
Finds bottom in the uncomprehensive deeps,
Keeps place with thought and almost, like the gods,
Does thoughts unveil in their dumb cradles.
There is a mystery—with whom relation
Durst never meddle—in the soul of state;
Which hath an operation more divine
Than breath or pen can give expressure to:
All the commerce that you have had with Troy
As perfectly is ours as yours, my lord;
And better would it fit Achilles much
To throw down Hector than Polyxena:
But it must grieve young Pyrrhus now at home,
When fame shall in our islands sound her trump,
And all the Greekish girls shall tripping sing,
'Great Hector's sister did Achilles win,
But our great Ajax bravely beat down him.'
Farewell, my lord: I as your lover speak;
The fool slides o'er the ice that you should break.

sentences.speaker: PATROCLUS
sentences.text:
To this effect, Achilles, have I moved you:
A woman impudent and mannish grown
Is not more loathed than an effeminate man
In time of action. I stand condemn'd for this;
They think my little stomach to the war
And your great love to me restrains you thus:
Sweet, rouse yourself; and the weak wanton Cupid
Shall from your neck unloose his amorous fold,
And, like a dew-drop from the lion's mane,
Be shook to air.

sentences.speaker: ACHILLES
sentences.text: Shall Ajax fight with Hector?

sentences.speaker: PATROCLUS
sentences.text: Ay, and perhaps receive much honour by him.

sentences.speaker: ACHILLES
sentences.text:
I see my reputation is at stake
My fame is shrewdly gored.

sentences.speaker: PATROCLUS
PATROCLUS
PATROCLUS
ACHILLES
sentences.text:
Go call Thersites hither, sweet Patroclus:
I'll send the fool to Ajax and desire him
To invite the Trojan lords after the combat
To see us here unarm'd: I have a woman's longing,
An appetite that I am sick withal,
To see great Hector in his weeds of peace,
To talk with him and to behold his visage,
Even to my full of view.

sentences.speaker: ACHILLES
sentences.text:
